1→# Slice: ActiveTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects all events into an ordered list of active
    10→(incomplete, non-deleted) tasks. This powers the main view of the application.
    11→
    12→## Diagram
    13→
    14→```mermaid
    15→flowchart LR
    16→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    17→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    18→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    19→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    20→    E5["TaskReprioritized"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E5 --> VIEW
    29→    E6 --> VIEW
    30→
    31→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    32→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    33→```
    34→
    35→## Read Model: ActiveTaskList
    36→
    37→### Fields
    38→
    39→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    40→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    41→| tasks | list | (all events) | Ordered list of active tasks |
    42→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    43→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    44→| tasks[].position | number | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | Current priority position |
    45→| completed_count | number | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1), TaskDeleted (if completed, -1) | Count of completed tasks for badge |
    46→
    47→### Filters
    48→
    49→- Only tasks where **completed = false** AND **deleted = false**.
    50→
    51→### Ordering
    52→
    53→- By position ascending. Position 1 = highest priority (top of list).
    54→
    55→### Event Processing Rules
    56→
    57→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    58→|-------|---------------------|
    59→| TaskAdded | Add new task at the bottom of the list (highest position number) |
    60→| TaskCompleted | Remove task from active list. Increment completed_count. |
    61→| TaskReopened | Add task back to active list at position 1 (top). Shift existing tasks down. Decrement completed_count. |
    62→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task. |
    63→| TaskReprioritized | Move task to new position. Shift other tasks to accommodate. |
    64→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from active list. If task was completed, decrement completed_count. |
    65→
    66→### Information Completeness Check
    67→
    68→| Display Element | Source |
    69→|----------------|--------|
    70→| Task description text | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    71→| Task order | TaskAdded.position / TaskReprioritized.position / TaskReopened (top) |
    72→| Completed count badge | TaskCompleted / TaskReopened / TaskDeleted |
    73→| Empty state message | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    74→
    75→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    76→
    77→## Wireframe
    78→
    79→```
    80→+--------------------------------------------------+
    81→|  My Tasks                        [Completed (3)]  |
    82→+--------------------------------------------------+
    83→|                                                    |
    84→|  [ What needs to be done?            ] [+ Add]    |
    85→|                                                    |
    86→|  ::  Buy groceries                    [v] [ x ]   |
    87→|  ::  Call the dentist                 [v] [ x ]   |
    88→|  ::  Fix the leaky faucet            [v] [ x ]   |
    89→|  ::  Write thank-you notes           [v] [ x ]   |
    90→|                                                    |
    91→+--------------------------------------------------+
    92→```
    93→
    94→## GWT Scenarios
    95→
    96→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of an empty list
    97→
    98→**Given** (current projection state):
    99→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [], completed_count: 0 }
   100→
   101→**When** (event to process):
   102→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }
   103→
   104→**Then** (resulting projection state):

   113→

   119→
   120→---

   123→
   124→**Given** (current projection state):

1→# Slice: CompletedTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects events into a list of completed (done, non-deleted)
    10→tasks. This powers the completed tasks view where users can review finished work
    11→and reopen or delete tasks.
    12→
    13→## Diagram
    14→
    15→```mermaid

    19→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    20→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["CompletedTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E6 --> VIEW
    29→
    30→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    31→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    32→```
    33→
    34→## Read Model: CompletedTaskList
    35→
    36→### Fields
    37→
    38→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    39→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    40→| tasks | list | (filtered events) | List of completed tasks |
    41→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    42→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    43→
    44→### Filters
    45→
    46→- Only tasks where **completed = true** AND **deleted = false**.
    47→
    48→### Ordering
    49→
    50→- By completion time descending (most recently completed first). Completion
    51→  time is derived from the TaskCompleted event timestamp.
    52→
    53→### Event Processing Rules
    54→
    55→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    56→|-------|---------------------|
    57→| TaskAdded | No effect (new tasks are not completed). |
    58→| TaskCompleted | Add task to completed list. |
    59→| TaskReopened | Remove task from completed list. |
    60→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task (if present in list). |
    61→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from completed list (if present). |
    62→
    63→### Information Completeness Check
    64→
    65→| Display Element | Source |
    66→|----------------|--------|
    67→| Task description text (with strikethrough) | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |

content.text:
I have read all 10 files. Now I will perform both the GWT Feedback evaluation and the Completeness Check.

---

## GWT Feedback Evaluation: Task Management

Analyzing 29 scenarios across 8 slices.

### 1. Checking Given Clauses

All Given clauses reference either:
- Empty state (no prior events)
- TaskAdded events (well-defined, with task_id, description, position)
- TaskCompleted events (well-defined, with task_id)
- TaskDescriptionChanged events (well-defined, with task_id, description)
- Read model projection states (for view slices)

Every state referenced in a Given clause traces back to a modeled event. No Given clause references state that requires unmodeled events.

### 2. Checking When Clauses

All When clauses reference either:
- AddTask command (defined)
- CompleteTask command (defined)
- ReopenTask command (defined)
- ChangeTaskDescription command (defined)
- ReprioritizeTask command (defined)
- DeleteTask command (defined)
- Events processed by read model projections (for view slices)

Every When clause maps to a defined command or event. No When clause implies a command or event that does not exist.

### 3. Checking Then Clauses

All Then clauses produce one of the six defined events or produce an error (rejection). No Then clause references an event that is not in the model. The error messages align with the rejection conditions documented on each command.

### 4. Checking Edge Case / Rejection Scenarios

Reviewing rejection coverage by command:

| Command | Rejection Scenarios Covered | Assessment |
|---------|----------------------------|------------|
| AddTask | Empty description handled by type system (NonEmptyString), documented as note | Adequate |
| CompleteTask | Already completed | Adequate |
| ReopenTask | Not completed (already active) | Adequate |
| ChangeTaskDescription | Same description (no change); empty handled by type system | Adequate |
| ReprioritizeTask | Same position (no change) | Adequate |
| DeleteTask | Cancellation is UI-only (command never issued), documented as note | Adequate |

Additional edge cases I checked:

- **Completing a deleted task**: The CompleteTask rejection conditions only check "already completed." Should completing a deleted task be rejected? The UI would never show a deleted task, so the command cannot be issued. This is adequate -- the UI prevents it.
- **Reopening a deleted-and-completed task**: Same reasoning -- the UI would not show it. No gap.
- **Reprioritizing a completed task**: Completed tasks have no drag handles per wireframe. UI prevents it. No gap.

## Summary

- Domain discovery establishing actors, goals, and business concepts for a single-user prioritized todo list
- Full 9-step event model for the Task Management workflow (the only workflow in this application)
- 33 GWT acceptance scenarios across 8 vertical slices

## Events

## Vertical Slices (8)

**Command (6):** AddTask, CompleteTask, ReopenTask, ChangeTaskDescription, ReprioritizeTask, DeleteTask
**View (2):** ActiveTaskList, CompletedTaskList

## Key Business Rules

- New tasks go to the bottom of the list (lowest priority)
- Reopened tasks go to the top of the list (highest priority)
- Completed tasks hidden from main view; visible in separate completed view
- Deletion requires confirmation; available from both views
- Inline description editing
- Single user, no authentication

## Review Focus

- Is the business process accurate?
- Are all events necessary and sufficient?
- Do the GWT scenarios cover edge cases?

**Note**: This is domain modeling only. No architecture decisions are included or requested.
EOF
)"

content.text:
# SDLC Design

Design event model workflows following Martin Dilger's "Understanding Eventsourcing" methodology and Adam Dymitruk's Event Modeling approach. Event modeling is about **understanding the business domain**, not documentation. The AI facilitates by asking probing questions, challenging assumptions, and keeping focus on business behavior - not technical implementation.

**CRITICAL**: No architecture or technical decisions during event modeling. Those belong to architecture design phase. The ONLY exception is mandatory third-party integrations (note name and purpose only).

## Arguments

`arch` may contain:
- `discover` - Start or continue domain discovery (required before first workflow)
- `workflow [name]` - Design a specific workflow (creates PR)
- `gwt <workflow-name>` - Generate GWT scenarios for a workflow
- `validate` - Validate the complete event model
- `arch` - Make architecture decisions (creates ARCHITECTURE.md with ADR PRs)
- `design-system` - Create/update the design system using Atomic Design methodology
- (no args) - Resume where you left off or start discovery

## Agent Invocation Pattern

All design phases use the Task tool with specialized agents:

```
Task tool with subagent_type="sdlc:<agent-name>":
  <instructions for the agent>
```

Available agents: `sdlc:discovery`, `sdlc:workflow-designer`, `sdlc:model-checker`, `sdlc:gwt`, `sdlc:design-facilitator`, `sdlc:ux`, `sdlc:architect`

## Steps

### 1. Check Configuration

Verify mode is `event-modeling` in `.claude/sdlc.yaml`.

If mode is `traditional`, inform user:
```
This project is configured for traditional development.
Event modeling design is for event-sourced applications.

To switch modes, edit .claude/sdlc.yaml or run /sdlc:setup again.
```

### 2. Check/Create Docs Structure

Check if event model docs exist:
```bash
ls docs/event_model/ 2>/dev/null
```

If not, create the structure:
```bash
mkdir -p docs/event_model/domain
mkdir -p docs/event_model/workflows
```

Create `docs/event_model/README.md` explaining the structure:
- `domain/` - Domain discovery overview
- `workflows/<name>/overview.md` - Workflow overview with master diagram
- `workflows/<name>/slices/` - Individual slice documents (each self-contained with GWT scenarios)

### 3. Check Auto Memory for Context

Use Grep to search for existing event modeling decisions:

```bash
MEMORY_PATH="$HOME/.claude/projects/$(pwd | sed 's/\//-/g' | sed 's/^-//')/memory"
grep -r -i "event model\|domain" "$MEMORY_PATH" --include="*.md" 2>/dev/null || true
```

Load any existing event modeling decisions or domain understanding.

### 4. Determine Action

Based on arguments:

#### `discover` or no args with no existing domain doc

**This phase establishes broad domain understanding WITHOUT diving deep into any single workflow.**

Use the `sdlc:discovery` agent to facilitate domain discovery:
- Who are the actors/users of the system?
- What are their high-level goals?
- What major business processes exist?
- What external systems must we integrate with?

Store results in `docs/event_model/domain/overview.md`. When complete, suggest the first workflow to model.

#### `workflow [name]`

**Each workflow design happens on its own branch and creates its own PR.**

For git-spice workflow, see the `shared/git-spice` skill.

If not using git-spice, create a fresh branch:
```bash
git checkout -b event-model/<workflow-name>
```

Use the `sdlc:workflow-designer` agent to guide through the 9-step event modeling process:
1. User Goal
2. Brainstorm Events
3. Order Events (The Plot)
4. Create Wireframes (The Storyboard)
5. Identify Commands
6. Design Read Models
7. Find Automations & Translations
8. Create Mermaid Diagram
9. Decompose into Slices

Output:
- `docs/event_model/workflows/<name>/overview.md`
- `docs/event_model/workflows/<name>/slices/<slice>.md` (one per slice)

After workflow design completes, use `sdlc:model-checker` in `COMPLETENESS_CHECK` mode. This is an ITERATIVE process - for each gap found, create the missing element immediately (ask user if needed), update the workflow document, and run the check AGAIN. Repeat until NO gaps remain.

#### `gwt <workflow>`

First verify we're on the correct workflow branch (`event-model/<workflow-name>`). If not, switch to it.

Use the `sdlc:gwt` agent to generate scenarios. **GWT structure depends on slice type** (see Appendix for pattern details):

- **Command slices**: Given=prior events, When=command, Then=events produced OR error
- **View slices**: Given=current projection state, When=new event, Then=resulting projection state

For each slice: identify pattern type, write happy path first, identify edge cases through questioning, write concrete scenarios with real example data, reference the wireframe in the slice document.

ADD scenarios to existing slice docs at `docs/event_model/workflows/<workflow>/slices/<slice>.md` (add `## GWT Scenarios` section).

After GWT scenarios, use `sdlc:model-checker` in `GWT_FEEDBACK` mode to evaluate if scenarios reveal missing workflow elements. For each gap, ask user to clarify business behavior, add missing element, update related elements.

Then run `sdlc:model-checker` in `COMPLETENESS_CHECK` mode again. Only after this check passes is the workflow ready for review.

#### `validate`

Use `sdlc:model-checker` in `VALIDATION` mode to check:
1. Information completeness - every read model attribute traces to an event
2. Event naming - past tense, business language
3. Command coverage - all events have triggering commands
4. Read model coverage - all queries have read models
5. Automation loops - no infinite event chains
6. Translation coverage - external data has anti-corruption layers

Report gaps as questions to resolve, not technical problems.

#### `arch`

**Prerequisites**:
- At least one workflow must be complete with GWT scenarios
- Domain discovery must exist (`docs/event_model/domain/overview.md`)

Check prerequisites:
```bash
test -f docs/event_model/domain/overview.md || echo "No domain discovery"
ls docs/event_model/workflows/*/overview.md 2>/dev/null || echo "No workflows"
```

If prerequisites not met, inform user what's missing and which commands to run first.

Use the `sdlc:design-facilitator` agent to facilitate architectural decisions:
1. Technology Stack
2. Domain Boundaries
3. Integration Approaches
4. Cross-Cutting Concerns

For EACH significant decision: present options with tradeoffs, after user decides use `/sdlc:adr decide <topic>`. This updates ARCHITECTURE.md directly and creates an ADR PR.

**Output**:
```
Architecture Design Complete: <project-name>

Architecture Document: docs/ARCHITECTURE.md (THE authoritative source)

Next step:
  /sdlc:plan - Create GitHub issues from event model slices

Note: Each decision has a corresponding ADR PR (labeled 'adr'). Merge to accept, close to reject.
```

#### `design-system`

**Prerequisites**:
- Architecture must exist (`docs/ARCHITECTURE.md`)
- At least one workflow must have wireframes

Check prerequisites:
```bash
test -f docs/ARCHITECTURE.md || echo "No architecture"
ls docs/event_model/workflows/*/overview.md 2>/dev/null || echo "No workflows"
```

If prerequisites not met, inform user to run `/sdlc:design arch` first.

Use the `sdlc:ux` agent in `DESIGN_SYSTEM` mode following Atomic Design process:
1. Analyze wireframes for common UI patterns
2. Gather visual style preferences from user
3. Create design tokens
4. Build component hierarchy (atoms -> molecules -> organisms -> templates)
5. Map read models to component data requirements

Output to `docs/design-system/`

### 5. After Workflow Design - Create/Update PR

After completing a workflow and its GWT scenarios:

```bash
git add docs/event_model/
git commit -m "event-model: add <workflow-name> workflow design and scenarios"
git push -u origin HEAD
gh pr create --title "Event Model: <Workflow Name>" \
  --body "## Workflow: <name>

### Events
<list from workflow doc>

### Vertical Slices
<list of slices>

### Review Focus
- Is the business process accurate?
- Are all events necessary and sufficient?
- Do the GWT scenarios cover edge cases?

**Note**: This is domain modeling only. No architecture decisions are included or requested."
```

For git-spice workflow, see the `shared/git-spice` skill.

### 6. Store in Auto Memory

After design work, use /sdlc:remember:

```bash
/sdlc:remember "<Project> <Workflow-Name> Event Model
Date: $(date +%Y-%m-%d)
Category: architecture
Project: <name> | Path: <path>
Events: <list of events>
Vertical Slices: <list of slices>
Status: <discovery|designed|scenarios-complete>"
```

### 7. Display Results

After domain discovery:
```
Domain Discovery Complete: <project-name>

Actors Identified:
  - <actor 1>: <brief description>
  - <actor 2>: <brief description>

Workflows Identified:
  - <workflow 1>: <brief description>
  - <workflow 2>: <brief description>

Suggested Starting Workflow: <name>
Reason: <why this workflow first>

Next step:
  /sdlc:design workflow <name>
```

After workflow design:
```
Workflow Designed: <name>
Branch: event-model/<name>

Documentation:
  docs/event_model/workflows/<name>/
  |- overview.md
  +- slices/
      |- <slice-1>.md
      |- <slice-2>.md
      +- ...

Vertical Slices: <count>
  Command: <list>
  View: <list>
  Automation: <list>
  Translation: <list>

Next steps:
  /sdlc:design gwt <name> - Generate GWT scenarios
  /sdlc:design workflow <next-name> - Design next workflow (stacked)
```

After GWT scenarios:
```
Scenarios Added: <workflow-name>

Slices updated:
  - <slice-1>.md: <n> scenarios
  - <slice-2>.md: <n> scenarios

PR ready for review.
```

After design system:
```
Design System Created: <project-name>

Documentation:
  docs/design-system/
  |- tokens.md
  |- atoms/
  |- molecules/
  |- organisms/
  |- templates/
  +- mappings.md

Components:
  Atoms: <count>
  Molecules: <count>
  Organisms: <count>
  Templates: <count>

Next step:
  /sdlc:plan - Create GitHub issues from event model slices
```

## What We Do NOT Discuss During Event Modeling

- Database choices
- API designs
- Programming languages
- Frameworks or libraries
- Message brokers or queues
- Deployment architecture
- Performance optimizations
- Caching strategies
- Any implementation detail

The ONLY exception: When a mandatory third-party integration exists, note its name and general purpose. Example: "Must integrate with Stripe for payments" - but NOT "We'll use webhooks with..." or "The API contract is..."

## Event Naming

Events are facts about the business, in business language:
- **Past tense**: Something that HAS happened
- **Business language**: A domain expert would understand
- **Immutable facts**: Not requests or intentions

Good: `OrderPlaced`, `PaymentReceived`, `CustomerRegistered`
Bad: `PlaceOrder`, `ProcessPayment`, `SaveCustomer`

## Appendix: The Four Patterns

These patterns describe business behavior, NOT technical architecture. **Each pattern = ONE vertical slice.**

1. **Command (State Change)**: Trigger -> Command -> Event(s)
   - GWT: Given=events, When=command, Then=events OR error
   - Color: White -> Blue -> Orange

2. **View (State View)**: Events -> Read Model
   - GWT: Given=projection state, When=event, Then=new state
   - Views CANNOT reject - they passively process events
   - Color: Orange -> Green

3. **Automation**: Event -> View (todo list) -> Process -> Command -> Event
   - GWT: Given=events, When=trigger event, Then=command + events
   - Color: Orange -> Green -> Purple -> Blue -> Orange

4. **Translation**: External Data -> Internal Event
   - GWT: Given=external state, When=external trigger, Then=internal event
   - Anti-corruption layer pattern
Let me verify the prerequisites first.

file.content:
# Domain Discovery: Todo List

## Purpose

The user needs a simple way to manage a prioritized list of next actions. The
application lets them capture tasks, organize them by importance, and track
what has been done -- all in one place with minimal friction.

This is a demonstration and learning project. The engineering complexity behind
it is intentional; the user-facing experience, however, should feel easy and
smooth.

## Actors

- **User** -- A single person managing their own task list. There is no
  authentication and no support for multiple users.

## User Goals

- See the current task list immediately upon opening the application.
- Add new tasks to the list.
- Edit the description of an existing task.
- Mark a task as complete.
- Set a completed task back to incomplete.
- Delete a task that is no longer needed.
- Reorder tasks by dragging and dropping them to express priority.

## Business Concepts

### Task

A task is a single item on the user's list. It has three attributes:

| Attribute        | Description                                                                 |
|------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| Description      | A short piece of text that says what needs to be done.                      |
| Completion state | Whether the task is done or not done.                                       |
| Position         | Where the task sits in the priority-ordered list (higher position = higher priority). |

Tasks are deliberately minimal. There are no due dates, categories, tags, or
notes.

### Task List

The task list is the single, ordered collection of all tasks. There is only one
list per application instance. Priority is expressed entirely through the
ordering of tasks in the list.

## External Integrations

None. The application is entirely self-contained with no third-party services.

## Workflows Identified

### Task Management

A single, unified workflow covering the full lifecycle of a task:

- **Creation** -- The user adds a new task to the list.
- **Viewing** -- The user sees all tasks and their current state.
- **Editing** -- The user changes a task's description.
- **Completion** -- The user marks a task as done.
- **Reopening** -- The user sets a completed task back to not done.
- **Reordering** -- The user drags a task to a new position to change its priority.
- **Deletion** -- The user removes a task from the list entirely.

## Suggested Starting Workflow

**Task Management** is the only workflow, so it is the natural starting point.
Within it, adding a task and viewing the task list should be modeled first,
since every other activity depends on tasks existing and being visible.

content.content:
1→# Workflow: Task Management 2→ 3→## Overview 4→ 5→A single, unified workflow covering the full lifecycle of a task in a 6→prioritized todo list. The user captures tasks, organizes them by priority 7→through drag-and-drop reordering, tracks completion, and removes tasks that are 8→no longer needed. 9→ 10→## User Goal 11→ 12→The user wants three things in equal measure: 13→ 14→1. **Rapid capture** -- quickly add tasks so nothing falls through the cracks. 15→2. **Clear prioritized visibility** -- always see an ordered list showing what 16→ to do next. 17→3. **Completion satisfaction** -- mark things done and watch the list shrink. 18→ 19→Success looks like: the user opens the app, immediately sees their prioritized 20→list, can add/edit/reorder/complete/delete tasks with minimal friction, and 21→leaves confident that everything important is tracked. 22→ 23→## Actors 24→ 25→- **User** -- A single person managing their own task list. There is no 26→ authentication and no support for multiple users. 27→ 28→## Events 29→ 30→| # | Event | Description | 31→|---|-------|-------------| 32→| 1 | TaskAdded | A new task was captured. Includes description and a default position at the bottom of the list. | 33→| 2 | TaskCompleted | A task was marked as done. It disappears from the active list. | 34→| 3 | TaskReopened | A completed task was set back to incomplete. It returns to the top of the active list (highest priority). | 35→| 4 | TaskDescriptionChanged | The description text of a task was edited. | 36→| 5 | TaskReprioritized | A task was moved to a new position in the list via drag and drop. | 37→| 6 | TaskDeleted | A task was permanently removed (after user confirmation). | 38→ 39→## Commands 40→ 41→| Command | Produces Event | Trigger | Inputs | Rejection Conditions | 42→|---------|---------------|---------|--------|---------------------| 43→| AddTask | TaskAdded | [+ Add] button or Enter key | description | Empty description | 44→| CompleteTask | TaskCompleted | Checkbox on active task | task_id | Already completed | 45→| ReopenTask | TaskReopened | [Reopen] button on completed task | task_id | Not completed | 46→| ChangeTaskDescription | TaskDescriptionChanged | Inline edit confirm (blur/Enter) | task_id, new_description | Empty or unchanged description | 47→| ReprioritizeTask | TaskReprioritized | Drag and drop to new position | task_id, new_position | Same position (no change) | 48→| DeleteTask | TaskDeleted | [x] button + confirmation dialog | task_id | User cancels confirmation | 49→ 50→## Read Models 51→ 52→### ActiveTaskList 53→ 54→Powers the main view. Answers: "What are my current tasks, in priority order?" 55→ 56→| Field | Source Event(s) | 57→|-------|----------------| 58→| tasks[].task_id | TaskAdded.task_id | 59→| tasks[].description | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | 60→| tasks[].position | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | 61→| completed_count | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1) | 62→ 63→**Filters:** Only tasks where completed = false AND deleted = false. 64→**Ordering:** By position ascending (highest priority first). 65→ 66→### CompletedTaskList 67→ 68→Powers the completed tasks view. Answers: "What tasks have I finished?" 69→ 70→| Field | Source Event(s) | 71→|-------|----------------| 72→| tasks[].task_id | TaskAdded.task_id | 73→| tasks[].description | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | 74→ 75→**Filters:** Only tasks where completed = true AND deleted = false. 76→**Ordering:** By completion time (most recently completed first), derived from 77→TaskCompleted event timestamp. 78→ 79→## Wireframes 80→ 81→### Active Task List (main view) 82→ 83→``` 84→+--------------------------------------------------+ 85→| My Tasks [Completed (3)] | 86→+--------------------------------------------------+ 87→| | 88→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 89→| | 90→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 91→| :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] | 92→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 93→| :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] | 94→| | 95→+--------------------------------------------------+ 96→ 97→Legend: 98→ :: = drag handle (for reordering via drag and drop) 99→ [v] = mark complete (checkbox) 100→ [x] = delete button 101→ [Completed (3)] = link to completed tasks view with count 102→``` 103→ 104→### Inline Editing 105→ 106→``` 107→+--------------------------------------------------+ 108→| My Tasks [Completed (3)] | 109→+--------------------------------------------------+ 110→| | 111→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 112→| | 113→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 114→| :: [Call the dentist office____] [v] [ x ] | 115→| ^--- inline editing active | 116→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 117→| :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] | 118→| | 119→+--------------------------------------------------+ 120→``` 121→ 122→### Delete Confirmation Dialog 123→ 124→``` 125→+--------------------------------------------------+ 126→| My Tasks [Completed (3)] | 127→+--------------------------------------------------+ 128→| | 129→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 130→| | Delete Task? | | 131→| | | | 132→| | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | 133→| | permanently removed. | | 134→| | | | 135→| | [Cancel] [Delete] | | 136→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 137→| | 138→+--------------------------------------------------+ 139→``` 140→ 141→### Completed Tasks View 142→ 143→``` 144→+--------------------------------------------------+ 145→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 146→+--------------------------------------------------+ 147→| | 148→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 149→| ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 150→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 151→| | 152→| (No drag handles -- completed tasks have no | 153→| priority order) | 154→| | 155→+--------------------------------------------------+ 156→ 157→Legend: 158→ ~~text~~ = strikethrough (visually indicates completion) 159→ [Reopen] = sets task back to incomplete, returns to top of active list 160→ [x] = delete button (with confirmation dialog) 161→``` 162→ 163→### Empty State 164→ 165→``` 166→+--------------------------------------------------+ 167→| My Tasks [Completed (0)] | 168→+--------------------------------------------------+ 169→| | 170→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 171→| | 172→| No tasks yet. Add one above! | 173→| | 174→+--------------------------------------------------+ 175→``` 176→ 177→## Automations 178→ 179→None. The application is entirely self-contained. 184→ 185→## Workflow Diagram 186→ 187→```mermaid 188→flowchart LR 189→ subgraph User["User"] 190→ ActiveView["Active Task List

fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 251→``` 252→ 253→## Vertical Slices 254→ 255→### Command Slices 256→ 257→1. [AddTask](slices/add-task.md) -- User adds a new task to the list 258→2. [CompleteTask](slices/complete-task.md) -- User marks a task as done 259→3. [ReopenTask](slices/reopen-task.md) -- User sets a completed task back to incomplete 260→4. [ChangeTaskDescription](slices/change-task-description.md) -- User edits a task's description 261→5. [ReprioritizeTask](slices/reprioritize-task.md) -- User reorders tasks via drag and drop 262→6. [DeleteTask](slices/delete-task.md) -- User permanently removes a task 263→ 264→### View Slices 265→ 266→7. [ActiveTaskList](slices/active-task-list.md) -- Read model for the main task list view 267→8. [CompletedTaskList](slices/completed-task-list.md) -- Read model for the completed tasks view 268→ 269→### Automation Slices 270→ 271→None. 272→ 273→### Translation Slices 274→ 275→None. 276→ 277→## Business Rules 278→ 279→- New tasks are always added to the bottom of the active list (lowest priority). 280→- Reopened tasks are always placed at the top of the active list (highest priority). 281→- Completed tasks are hidden from the active list and visible in a separate completed view. 282→- Deletion requires user confirmation via a dialog. 283→- Deleted tasks cannot be restored from the UI (though events are preserved in the stream). 284→- Tasks can be deleted from either the active list or the completed tasks view. 285→- Task descriptions cannot be empty. 286→ 287→## Key Design Decisions 288→ 289→| Decision | Rationale | 290→|----------|-----------| 291→| New tasks go to bottom | Avoids disrupting existing priority order; user can reprioritize afterward | 292→| Reopened tasks go to top | If re-opening, the task is likely urgent or important | 293→| Completed tasks hidden by default | Keeps main list focused on what needs doing | 294→| Separate completed view | Allows review and reopening without cluttering the active list | 295→| Delete confirmation required | Prevents accidental permanent removal | 296→| Delete available in both views | User should not have to navigate between views to clean up | 297→| Inline description editing | Minimal friction; no modal or separate screen needed | 298→| Single list, no categories | Deliberately minimal; priority expressed only through ordering | 299→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

# Workflow: Task Management ## Overview A single, unified workflow covering the full lifecycle of a task in a prioritized todo list. The user captures tasks, organizes them by priority through drag-and-drop reordering, tracks completion, and removes tasks that are no longer needed. ## User Goal The user wants three things in equal measure: 1. **Rapid capture** -- quickly add tasks so nothing falls through the cracks. 2. **Clear prioritized visibility** -- always see an ordered list showing what to do next. 3. **Completion satisfaction** -- mark things done and watch the list shrink. Success looks like: the user opens the app, immediately sees their prioritized list, can add/edit/reorder/complete/delete tasks with minimal friction, and leaves confident that everything important is tracked. ## Actors - **User** -- A single person managing their own task list. There is no authentication and no support for multiple users. ## Events | # | Event | Description | |---|-------|-------------| | 1 | TaskAdded | A new task was captured. Includes description and a default position at the bottom of the list. | | 2 | TaskCompleted | A task was marked as done. It disappears from the active list. | | 3 | TaskReopened | A completed task was set back to incomplete. It returns to the top of the active list (highest priority). | | 4 | TaskDescriptionChanged | The description text of a task was edited. | | 5 | TaskReprioritized | A task was moved to a new position in the list via drag and drop. | | 6 | TaskDeleted | A task was permanently removed (after user confirmation). | ## Commands | Command | Produces Event | Trigger | Inputs | Rejection Conditions | |---------|---------------|---------|--------|---------------------| | AddTask | TaskAdded | [+ Add] button or Enter key | description | Empty description | | CompleteTask | TaskCompleted | Checkbox on active task | task_id | Already completed | | ReopenTask | TaskReopened | [Reopen] button on completed task | task_id | Not completed | | ChangeTaskDescription | TaskDescriptionChanged | Inline edit confirm (blur/Enter) | task_id, new_description | Empty or unchanged description | | ReprioritizeTask | TaskReprioritized | Drag and drop to new position | task_id, new_position | Same position (no change) | | DeleteTask | TaskDeleted | [x] button + confirmation dialog | task_id | User cancels confirmation | ## Read Models ### ActiveTaskList Powers the main view. Answers: "What are my current tasks, in priority order?" | Field | Source Event(s) | |-------|----------------| | tasks[].task_id | TaskAdded.task_id | | tasks[].description | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | | tasks[].position | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | | completed_count | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1) | **Filters:** Only tasks where completed = false AND deleted = false. **Ordering:** By position ascending (highest priority first). ### CompletedTaskList Powers the completed tasks view. Answers: "What tasks have I finished?" | Field | Source Event(s) | |-------|----------------| | tasks[].task_id | TaskAdded.task_id | | tasks[].description | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | **Filters:** Only tasks where completed = true AND deleted = false. **Ordering:** By completion time (most recently completed first), derived from TaskCompleted event timestamp. ## Wireframes ### Active Task List (main view) ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (3)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | | | | :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | | :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] | | :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | | :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ Legend: :: = drag handle (for reordering via drag and drop) [v] = mark complete (checkbox) [x] = delete button [Completed (3)] = link to completed tasks view with count ``` ### Inline Editing ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (3)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | | | | :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | | :: [Call the dentist office____] [v] [ x ] | | ^--- inline editing active | | :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | | :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ ``` ### Delete Confirmation Dialog ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (3)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | +--------------------------------------------+ | | | Delete Task? | | | | | | | | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | | | permanently removed. | | | | | | | | [Cancel] [Delete] | | | +--------------------------------------------+ | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ ``` ### Completed Tasks View ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | | | | (No drag handles -- completed tasks have no | | priority order) | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ Legend: ~~text~~ = strikethrough (visually indicates completion) [Reopen] = sets task back to incomplete, returns to top of active list [x] = delete button (with confirmation dialog) ``` ### Empty State ``` +--------------------------------------------------+ | My Tasks [Completed (0)] | +--------------------------------------------------+ | | | [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | | | | No tasks yet. Add one above! | | | +--------------------------------------------------+ ``` ## Automations None. This is a purely user-driven workflow with no automatic reactions. ## Translations (External Integrations) None. The application is entirely self-contained. ## Workflow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph User["User"] ActiveView["Active Task List
(main view)"] CompletedView["Completed Tasks
(separate view)"] ConfirmDialog["Delete Confirmation
Dialog"] end subgraph Commands["Commands"] AddTask["AddTask"]:::command CompleteTask["CompleteTask"]:::command ReopenTask["ReopenTask"]:::command ChangeDesc["ChangeTaskDescription"]:::command Reprioritize["ReprioritizeTask"]:::command DeleteTask["DeleteTask"]:::command end subgraph Events["Events"] TaskAdded["TaskAdded"]:::event TaskCompleted["TaskCompleted"]:::event TaskReopened["TaskReopened"]:::event TaskDescChanged["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event TaskReprioritized["TaskReprioritized"]:::event TaskDeleted["TaskDeleted"]:::event end subgraph Views["Read Models"] ActiveList["ActiveTaskList"]:::view CompletedList["CompletedTaskList"]:::view end ActiveView -->|"type + Add"| AddTask ActiveView -->|"click checkbox"| CompleteTask ActiveView -->|"inline edit"| ChangeDesc ActiveView -->|"drag and drop"| Reprioritize ActiveView -->|"click x"| ConfirmDialog CompletedView -->|"click Reopen"| ReopenTask CompletedView -->|"click x"| ConfirmDialog ConfirmDialog -->|"confirm"| DeleteTask AddTask --> TaskAdded CompleteTask --> TaskCompleted ReopenTask --> TaskReopened ChangeDesc --> TaskDescChanged Reprioritize --> TaskReprioritized DeleteTask --> TaskDeleted TaskAdded --> ActiveList TaskCompleted --> ActiveList TaskCompleted --> CompletedList TaskReopened --> ActiveList TaskReopened --> CompletedList TaskDescChanged --> ActiveList TaskDescChanged --> CompletedList TaskReprioritized --> ActiveList TaskDeleted --> ActiveList TaskDeleted --> CompletedList ActiveList --> ActiveView CompletedList --> CompletedView classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff ``` ## Vertical Slices ### Command Slices 1. [AddTask](slices/add-task.md) -- User adds a new task to the list 2. [CompleteTask](slices/complete-task.md) -- User marks a task as done 3. [ReopenTask](slices/reopen-task.md) -- User sets a completed task back to incomplete 4. [ChangeTaskDescription](slices/change-task-description.md) -- User edits a task's description 5. [ReprioritizeTask](slices/reprioritize-task.md) -- User reorders tasks via drag and drop 6. [DeleteTask](slices/delete-task.md) -- User permanently removes a task ### View Slices 7. [ActiveTaskList](slices/active-task-list.md) -- Read model for the main task list view 8. [CompletedTaskList](slices/completed-task-list.md) -- Read model for the completed tasks view ### Automation Slices None. ### Translation Slices None. ## Business Rules - New tasks are always added to the bottom of the active list (lowest priority). - Reopened tasks are always placed at the top of the active list (highest priority). - Completed tasks are hidden from the active list and visible in a separate completed view. - Deletion requires user confirmation via a dialog. - Deleted tasks cannot be restored from the UI (though events are preserved in the stream). - Tasks can be deleted from either the active list or the completed tasks view. - Task descriptions cannot be empty. ## Key Design Decisions | Decision | Rationale | |----------|-----------| | New tasks go to bottom | Avoids disrupting existing priority order; user can reprioritize afterward | | Reopened tasks go to top | If re-opening, the task is likely urgent or important | | Completed tasks hidden by default | Keeps main list focused on what needs doing | | Separate completed view | Allows review and reopening without cluttering the active list | | Delete confirmation required | Prevents accidental permanent removal | | Delete available in both views | User should not have to navigate between views to clean up | | Inline description editing | Minimal friction; no modal or separate screen needed | | Single list, no categories | Deliberately minimal; priority expressed only through ordering |

1→This is a web application written using the Phoenix web framework. 2→ 3→## Development Environment 4→ 5→This project uses a Nix-based development environment for reproducible Elixir/Phoenix development. 6→ 7→The dev shell should already be loaded via direnv. If not, run `nix develop`. 8→ 9→### Running utilities not in the dev shell 10→ 11→Use `nix shell` to temporarily run utilities: 12→```bash 13→nix shell nixpkgs# -c 14→``` 15→ 16→### Universal Tools Available 17→ 18→- `just` - Task runner (see Justfile if present) 19→- `glow` - Render markdown in terminal (`glow README.md`) 20→- `jq` - JSON processing 21→- `git-spice` - Stacked PR workflow (`gs` commands) 22→- `pre-commit` - Git hooks 23→ 24→### Language-Specific Tools 25→ 26→**Elixir/Phoenix:** 27→- `elixir` - Elixir runtime 28→- `mix` - Build tool and task runner 29→- `iex` - Interactive Elixir shell 30→- `elixir-ls` - Language server for IDE integration 31→- `psql` - PostgreSQL client (version 17) 32→ 33→**Container Tools:** 34→- `docker` - Container runtime 35→- `docker-compose` - Multi-container orchestration 36→ 37→**Note on Language Servers:** 38→Consider using [Lexical](https://github.com/lexical-lsp/lexical) instead of ElixirLS for better performance and features. Lexical can be built from source within this Nix environment. 39→ 40→## Database Setup 41→ 42→PostgreSQL 17 runs in Docker via docker-compose. 43→ 44→**Connection parameters:** 45→- **Host:** localhost 46→- **Port:** 5432 47→- **User:** postgres 48→- **Password:** postgres 49→- **Database:** todo_list_dev 50→ 51→Phoenix database URL format: 52→``` 53→DATABASE_URL=postgres://postgres:postgres@localhost:5432/todo_list_dev 54→``` 55→ 56→**Docker commands:** 57→```bash 58→# Start PostgreSQL 59→docker-compose up -d 60→ 61→# Check status 62→docker-compose ps 63→ 64→# View logs 65→docker-compose logs -f postgres 66→ 67→# Stop PostgreSQL 68→docker-compose down 69→ 70→# Stop and remove volumes (DESTRUCTIVE) 71→docker-compose down -v 72→``` 73→ 74→## Common Mix Tasks 75→ 76→```bash 77→# Get dependencies 78→mix deps.get 79→ 80→# Compile project 81→mix compile 82→ 83→# Run tests 84→mix test 85→ 86→# Run tests with coverage 87→mix test --cover 88→ 89→# Database tasks 90→mix ecto.create # Create database 91→mix ecto.migrate # Run migrations 92→mix ecto.rollback # Rollback last migration 93→mix ecto.reset # Drop, create, and migrate 94→ 95→# Code quality 96→mix format # Format code 97→mix credo # Static analysis (if added) 98→mix dialyzer # Type checking (if added) 99→ 100→# Interactive shell 101→iex -S mix # Start IEx with project loaded 102→iex -S mix phx.server # Start server with IEx shell 103→``` 104→ 105→## Git Workflow 106→ 107→This project uses git-spice for stacked PRs: 108→- `gs repo sync` - Sync with remote 109→- `gs stack submit` - Submit all stacked PRs 110→- `gs branch create ` - Create new stacked branch 111→- `gs upstack onto ` - Restack commits onto branch 112→ 113→## Commit Message Guidelines 114→ 115→Use commitizen-style commit messages: 116→ 117→``` 118→(): 119→ 120→ 121→``` 122→ 123→**Types:** feat, fix, docs, style, refactor, perf, test, build, ci, chore, revert 124→ 125→**Examples:** 126→- `feat(users): add user registration endpoint` 127→- `fix(auth): resolve token expiration bug` 128→- `docs(readme): update setup instructions` 129→ 130→Commit messages should explain the **WHY**, not just what changed. 131→ 132→## Troubleshooting 133→ 134→### Dev shell not loading 135→```bash 136→# Check direnv status 137→direnv status 138→ 139→# Force reload 140→direnv allow 141→ 142→# Or manually enter shell 143→nix develop 144→``` 145→ 146→### Mix/Hex issues 147→```bash 148→# Clean Mix cache 149→rm -rf .nix-shell/mix .nix-shell/hex 150→ 151→# Reload shell (will reinstall hex/rebar3) 152→direnv reload 153→ 154→# Or manually reinstall 155→mix local.hex --force 156→mix local.rebar --force 157→``` 158→ 159→### PostgreSQL connection issues 160→```bash 161→# Check if PostgreSQL is running 162→docker-compose ps 163→ 164→# Check PostgreSQL logs 165→docker-compose logs postgres 166→ 167→# Restart PostgreSQL 168→docker-compose restart postgres 169→``` 170→ 171→### Dependency conflicts 172→```bash 173→# Clean all dependencies 174→mix deps.clean --all 175→ 176→# Get fresh dependencies 177→mix deps.get 178→ 179→# Compile everything 180→mix deps.compile 181→``` 182→ 183→## Additional Resources 184→ 185→- [Elixir Documentation](https://elixir-lang.org/docs.html) 186→- [Phoenix Framework](https://www.phoenixframework.org/) 187→- [Phoenix LiveView](https://hexdocs.pm/phoenix_live_view/) 188→- [Ecto Documentation](https://hexdocs.pm/ecto/) 189→- [Elixir Forum](https://elixirforum.com/) 190→ 191→## Project guidelines 192→ 193→- Use `mix precommit` alias when you are done with all changes and fix any pending issues 194→- Use the already included and available `:req` (`Req`) library for HTTP requests, **avoid** `:httpoison`, `:tesla`, and `:httpc`. Req is included by default and is the preferred HTTP client for Phoenix apps 195→ 196→### Phoenix v1.8 guidelines 197→ 198→- **Always** begin your LiveView templates with `` which wraps all inner content 199→- The `MyAppWeb.Layouts` module is aliased in the `my_app_web.ex` file, so you can use it without needing to alias it again 200→- Anytime you run into errors with no `current_scope` assign: 201→ - You failed to follow the Authenticated Routes guidelines, or you failed to pass `current_scope` to `` 202→ - **Always** fix the `current_scope` error by moving your routes to the proper `live_session` and ensure you pass `current_scope` as needed 203→- Phoenix v1.8 moved the `<.flash_group>` component to the `Layouts` module. You are **forbidden** from calling `<.flash_group>` outside of the `layouts.ex` module 204→- Out of the box, `core_components.ex` imports an `<.icon name="hero-x-mark" class="w-5 h-5"/>` component for for hero icons. **Always** use the `<.icon>` component for icons, **never** use `Heroicons` modules or similar 205→- **Always** use the imported `<.input>` component for form inputs from `core_components.ex` when available. `<.input>` is imported and using it will save steps and prevent errors 206→- If you override the default input classes (`<.input class="myclass px-2 py-1 rounded-lg">)`) class with your own values, no default classes are inherited, so your 207→custom classes must fully style the input 208→ 209→### JS and CSS guidelines 210→ 211→- **Use Tailwind CSS classes and custom CSS rules** to create polished, responsive, and visually stunning interfaces. 212→- Tailwindcss v4 **no longer needs a tailwind.config.js** and uses a new import syntax in `app.css`: 213→ 214→ @import "tailwindcss" source(none); 215→ @source "../css"; 216→ @source "../js"; 217→ @source "../../lib/my_app_web"; 218→ 219→- **Always use and maintain this import syntax** in the app.css file for projects generated with `phx.new` 220→- **Never** use `@apply` when writing raw css 221→- **Always** manually write your own tailwind-based components instead of using daisyUI for a unique, world-class design 222→- Out of the box **only the app.js and app.css bundles are supported** 223→ - You cannot reference an external vendor'd script `src` or link `href` in the layouts 224→ - You must import the vendor deps into app.js and app.css to use them 225→ - **Never write inline tags within templates** 226→ 227→### UI/UX & design guidelines 228→ 229→- **Produce world-class UI designs** with a focus on usability, aesthetics, and modern design principles 230→- Implement **subtle micro-interactions** (e.g., button hover effects, and smooth transitions) 231→- Ensure **clean typography, spacing, and layout balance** for a refined, premium look 232→- Focus on **delightful details** like hover effects, loading states, and smooth page transitions 233→ 234→ 235→ 236→ 237→ 238→## Elixir guidelines 239→ 240→- Elixir lists **do not support index based access via the access syntax** 241→ 242→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 243→ 244→ i = 0 245→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 246→ mylist[i] 247→ 248→ Instead, **always** use `Enum.at`, pattern matching, or `List` for index based list access, ie: 249→ 250→ i = 0 251→ mylist = ["blue", "green"] 252→ Enum.at(mylist, i) 253→ 254→- Elixir variables are immutable, but can be rebound, so for block expressions like `if`, `case`, `cond`, etc 255→ you *must* bind the result of the expression to a variable if you want to use it and you CANNOT rebind the result inside the expression, ie: 256→ 257→ # INVALID: we are rebinding inside the `if` and the result never gets assigned 258→ if connected?(socket) do 259→ socket = assign(socket, :val, val) 260→ end 261→ 262→ # VALID: we rebind the result of the `if` to a new variable 263→ socket = 264→ if connected?(socket) do 265→ assign(socket, :val, val) 266→ end 267→ 268→- **Never** nest multiple modules in the same file as it can cause cyclic dependencies and compilation errors 269→- **Never** use map access syntax (`changeset[:field]`) on structs as they do not implement the Access behaviour by default. For regular structs, you **must** access the fields directly, such as `my_struct.field` or use higher level APIs that are available on the struct if they exist, `Ecto.Changeset.get_field/2` for changesets 270→- Elixir's standard library has everything necessary for date and time manipulation. Familiarize yourself with the common `Time`, `Date`, `DateTime`, and `Calendar` interfaces by accessing their documentation as necessary. **Never** install additional dependencies unless asked or for date/time parsing (which you can use the `date_time_parser` package) 271→- Don't use `String.to_atom/1` on user input (memory leak risk) 272→- Predicate function names should not start with `is_` and should end in a question mark. Names like `is_thing` should be reserved for guards 273→- Elixir's builtin OTP primitives like `DynamicSupervisor` and `Registry`, require names in the child spec, such as `{DynamicSupervisor, name: MyApp.MyDynamicSup}`, then you can use `DynamicSupervisor.start_child(MyApp.MyDynamicSup, child_spec)` 274→- Use `Task.async_stream(collection, callback, options)` for concurrent enumeration with back-pressure. The majority of times you will want to pass `timeout: :infinity` as option 275→ 276→## Mix guidelines 277→ 278→- Read the docs and options before using tasks (by using `mix help task_name`) 279→- To debug test failures, run tests in a specific file with `mix test test/my_test.exs` or run all previously failed tests with `mix test --failed` 280→- `mix deps.clean --all` is **almost never needed**. **Avoid** using it unless you have good reason 281→ 282→## Test guidelines 283→ 284→- **Always use `start_supervised!/1`** to start processes in tests as it guarantees cleanup between tests 285→- **Avoid** `Process.sleep/1` and `Process.alive?/1` in tests 286→ - Instead of sleeping to wait for a process to finish, **always** use `Process.monitor/1` and assert on the DOWN message: 287→ 288→ ref = Process.monitor(pid) 289→ assert_receive {:DOWN, ^ref, :process, ^pid, :normal} 290→ 291→ - Instead of sleeping to synchronize before the next call, **always** use `_ = :sys.get_state/1` to ensure the process has handled prior messages 292→ 293→ 294→ 295→## Phoenix guidelines 296→ 297→- Remember Phoenix router `scope` blocks include an optional alias which is prefixed for all routes within the scope. **Always** be mindful of this when creating routes within a scope to avoid duplicate module prefixes. 298→ 299→- You **never** need to create your own `alias` for route definitions! The `scope` provides the alias, ie: 300→ 301→ scope "/admin", AppWeb.Admin do 302→ pipe_through :browser 303→ 304→ live "/users", UserLive, :index 305→ end 306→ 307→ the UserLive route would point to the `AppWeb.Admin.UserLive` module 308→ 309→- `Phoenix.View` no longer is needed or included with Phoenix, don't use it 310→ 311→ 312→ 313→## Ecto Guidelines 314→ 315→- **Always** preload Ecto associations in queries when they'll be accessed in templates, ie a message that needs to reference the `message.user.email` 316→- Remember `import Ecto.Query` and other supporting modules when you write `seeds.exs` 317→- `Ecto.Schema` fields always use the `:string` type, even for `:text`, columns, ie: `field :name, :string` 318→- `Ecto.Changeset.validate_number/2` **DOES NOT SUPPORT the `:allow_nil` option**. By default, Ecto validations only run if a change for the given field exists and the change value is not nil, so such as option is never needed 319→- You **must** use `Ecto.Changeset.get_field(changeset, :field)` to access changeset fields 320→- Fields which are set programatically, such as `user_id`, must not be listed in `cast` calls or similar for security purposes. Instead they must be explicitly set when creating the struct 321→- **Always** invoke `mix ecto.gen.migration migration_name_using_underscores` when generating migration files, so the correct timestamp and conventions are applied 322→ 323→ 324→ 325→## Phoenix HTML guidelines 326→ 327→- Phoenix templates **always** use `~H` or .html.heex files (known as HEEx), **never** use `~E` 328→- **Always** use the imported `Phoenix.Component.form/1` and `Phoenix.Component.inputs_for/1` function to build forms. **Never** use `Phoenix.HTML.form_for` or `Phoenix.HTML.inputs_for` as they are outdated 329→- When building forms **always** use the already imported `Phoenix.Component.to_form/2` (`assign(socket, form: to_form(...))` and `<.form for={@form} id="msg-form">`), then access those forms in the template via `@form[:field]` 330→- **Always** add unique DOM IDs to key elements (like forms, buttons, etc) when writing templates, these IDs can later be used in tests (`<.form for={@form} id="product-form">`) 331→- For "app wide" template imports, you can import/alias into the `my_app_web.ex`'s `html_helpers` block, so they will be available to all LiveViews, LiveComponent's, and all modules that do `use MyAppWeb, :html` (replace "my_app" by the actual app name) 332→ 333→- Elixir supports `if/else` but **does NOT support `if/else if` or `if/elsif`**. **Never use `else if` or `elseif` in Elixir**, **always** use `cond` or `case` for multiple conditionals. 334→ 335→ **Never do this (invalid)**: 336→ 337→ <%= if condition do %> 338→ ... 339→ <% else if other_condition %> 340→ ... 341→ <% end %> 342→ 343→ Instead **always** do this: 344→ 345→ <%= cond do %> 346→ <% condition -> %> 347→ ... 348→ <% condition2 -> %> 349→ ... 350→ <% true -> %> 351→ ... 352→ <% end %> 353→ 354→- HEEx require special tag annotation if you want to insert literal curly's like `{` or `}`. If you want to show a textual code snippet on the page in a `
` or `` block you *must* annotate the parent tag with `phx-no-curly-interpolation`:
   355→
   356→      
   357→        let obj = {key: "val"}
   358→      
   359→
   360→  Within `phx-no-curly-interpolation` annotated tags, you can use `{` and `}` without escaping them, and dynamic Elixir expressions can still be used with `<%= ... %>` syntax
   361→
   362→- HEEx class attrs support lists, but you must **always** use list `[...]` syntax. You can use the class list syntax to conditionally add classes, **always do this for multiple class values**:
   363→
   364→      Text
   370→
   371→  and **always** wrap `if`'s inside `{...}` expressions with parens, like done above (`if(@other_condition, do: "...", else: "...")`)
   372→
   373→  and **never** do this, since it's invalid (note the missing `[` and `]`):
   374→
   375→       ...
   379→      => Raises compile syntax error on invalid HEEx attr syntax
   380→
   381→- **Never** use `<% Enum.each %>` or non-for comprehensions for generating template content, instead **always** use `<%= for item <- @collection do %>`
   382→- HEEx HTML comments use `<%!-- comment --%>`. **Always** use the HEEx HTML comment syntax for template comments (`<%!-- comment --%>`)
   383→- HEEx allows interpolation via `{...}` and `<%= ... %>`, but the `<%= %>` **only** works within tag bodies. **Always** use the `{...}` syntax for interpolation within tag attributes, and for interpolation of values within tag bodies. **Always** interpolate block constructs (if, cond, case, for) within tag bodies using `<%= ... %>`.
   384→
   385→  **Always** do this:
   386→
   387→      

   388→        {@my_assign}
   389→        <%= if @some_block_condition do %>
   390→          {@another_assign}
   391→        <% end %>
   392→      

   393→
   394→  and **Never** do this – the program will terminate with a syntax error:
   395→
   396→      <%!-- THIS IS INVALID NEVER EVER DO THIS --%>
   397→      

   398→        {if @invalid_block_construct do}
   399→        {end}
   400→      

   401→
   402→
   403→
   404→## Phoenix LiveView guidelines
   405→
   406→- **Never** use the deprecated `live_redirect` and `live_patch` functions, instead **always** use the `<.link navigate={href}>` and  `<.link patch={href}>` in templates, and `push_navigate` and `push_patch` functions LiveViews
   407→- **Avoid LiveComponent's** unless you have a strong, specific need for them
   408→- LiveViews should be named like `AppWeb.WeatherLive`, with a `Live` suffix. When you go to add LiveView routes to the router, the default `:browser` scope is **already aliased** with the `AppWeb` module, so you can just do `live "/weather", WeatherLive`
   409→
   410→### LiveView streams
   411→
   412→- **Always** use LiveView streams for collections for assigning regular lists to avoid memory ballooning and runtime termination with the following operations:
   413→  - basic append of N items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])`
   414→  - resetting stream with new items - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], reset: true)` (e.g. for filtering items)
   415→  - prepend to stream - `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg], at: -1)`
   416→  - deleting items - `stream_delete(socket, :messages, msg)`
   417→
   418→- When using the `stream/3` interfaces in the LiveView, the LiveView template must 1) always set `phx-update="stream"` on the parent element, with a DOM id on the parent element like `id="messages"` and 2) consume the `@streams.stream_name` collection and use the id as the DOM id for each child. For a call like `stream(socket, :messages, [new_msg])` in the LiveView, the template would be:
   419→
   420→      

   421→        

   422→          {msg.text}
   423→        

   424→      

   425→
   426→- LiveView streams are *not* enumerable, so you cannot use `Enum.filter/2` or `Enum.reject/2` on them. Instead, if you want to filter, prune, or refresh a list of items on the UI, you **must refetch the data and re-stream the entire stream collection, passing reset: true**:
   427→
   428→      def handle_event("filter", %{"filter" => filter}, socket) do
   429→        # re-fetch the messages based on the filter
   430→        messages = list_messages(filter)
   431→
   432→        {:noreply,
   433→         socket
   434→         |> assign(:messages_empty?, messages == [])
   435→         # reset the stream with the new messages
   436→         |> stream(:messages, messages, reset: true)}
   437→      end
   438→
   439→- LiveView streams *do not support counting or empty states*. If you need to display a count, you must track it using a separate assign. For empty states, you can use Tailwind classes:
   440→
   441→      

   442→        
No tasks yet

   443→        

   444→          {task.name}
   445→        

   446→      

   447→
   448→  The above only works if the empty state is the only HTML block alongside the stream for-comprehension.
   449→
   450→- When updating an assign that should change content inside any streamed item(s), you MUST re-stream the items
   451→  along with the updated assign:
   452→
   453→      def handle_event("edit_message", %{"message_id" => message_id}, socket) do
   454→        message = Chat.get_message!(message_id)
   455→        edit_form = to_form(Chat.change_message(message, %{content: message.content}))
   456→
   457→        # re-insert message so @editing_message_id toggle logic takes effect for that stream item
   458→        {:noreply,
   459→         socket
   460→         |> stream_insert(:messages, message)
   461→         |> assign(:editing_message_id, String.to_integer(message_id))
   462→         |> assign(:edit_form, edit_form)}
   463→      end
   464→
   465→  And in the template:
   466→
   467→      

   468→        

   469→          {message.username}
   470→          <%= if @editing_message_id == message.id do %>
   471→            <%!-- Edit mode --%>
   472→            <.form for={@edit_form} id="edit-form-#{message.id}" phx-submit="save_edit">
   473→              ...
   474→            
   475→          <% end %>
   476→        

   477→      

   478→
   479→- **Never** use the deprecated `phx-update="append"` or `phx-update="prepend"` for collections
   480→
   481→### LiveView JavaScript interop
   482→
   483→- Remember anytime you use `phx-hook="MyHook"` and that JS hook manages its own DOM, you **must** also set the `phx-update="ignore"` attribute
   484→- **Always** provide an unique DOM id alongside `phx-hook` otherwise a compiler error will be raised
   485→
   486→LiveView hooks come in two flavors, 1) colocated js hooks for "inline" scripts defined inside HEEx,
   487→and 2) external `phx-hook` annotations where JavaScript object literals are defined and passed to the `LiveSocket` constructor.
   488→
   489→#### Inline colocated js hooks
   490→
   491→**Never** write raw embedded `
   508→
   509→- colocated hooks are automatically integrated into the app.js bundle
   510→- colocated hooks names **MUST ALWAYS** start with a `.` prefix, i.e. `.PhoneNumber`
   511→
   512→#### External phx-hook
   513→
   514→External JS hooks (`
`) must be placed in `assets/js/` and passed to the
   515→LiveSocket constructor:
   516→
   517→    const MyHook = {
   518→      mounted() { ... }
   519→    }
   520→    let liveSocket = new LiveSocket("/live", Socket, {
   521→      hooks: { MyHook }
   522→    });
   523→
   524→#### Pushing events between client and server
   525→
   526→Use LiveView's `push_event/3` when you need to push events/data to the client for a phx-hook to handle.
   527→**Always** return or rebind the socket on `push_event/3` when pushing events:
   528→
   529→    # re-bind socket so we maintain event state to be pushed
   530→    socket = push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})
   531→
   532→    # or return the modified socket directly:
   533→    def handle_event("some_event", _, socket) do
   534→      {:noreply, push_event(socket, "my_event", %{...})}
   535→    end
   536→
   537→Pushed events can then be picked up in a JS hook with `this.handleEvent`:
   538→
   539→    mounted() {
   540→      this.handleEvent("my_event", data => console.log("from server:", data));
   541→    }
   542→
   543→Clients can also push an event to the server and receive a reply with `this.pushEvent`:
   544→
   545→    mounted() {
   546→      this.el.addEventListener("click", e => {
   547→        this.pushEvent("my_event", { one: 1 }, reply => console.log("got reply from server:", reply));
   548→      })
   549→    }
   550→
   551→Where the server handled it via:
   552→
   553→    def handle_event("my_event", %{"one" => 1}, socket) do
   554→      {:reply, %{two: 2}, socket}
   555→    end
   556→
   557→### LiveView tests
   558→
   559→- `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` module and `LazyHTML` (included) for making your assertions
   560→- Form tests are driven by `Phoenix.LiveViewTest`'s `render_submit/2` and `render_change/2` functions
   561→- Come up with a step-by-step test plan that splits major test cases into small, isolated files. You may start with simpler tests that verify content exists, gradually add interaction tests
   562→- **Always reference the key element IDs you added in the LiveView templates in your tests** for `Phoenix.LiveViewTest` functions like `element/2`, `has_element/2`, selectors, etc
   563→- **Never** tests again raw HTML, **always** use `element/2`, `has_element/2`, and similar: `assert has_element?(view, "#my-form")`
   564→- Instead of relying on testing text content, which can change, favor testing for the presence of key elements
   565→- Focus on testing outcomes rather than implementation details
   566→- Be aware that `Phoenix.Component` functions like `<.form>` might produce different HTML than expected. Test against the output HTML structure, not your mental model of what you expect it to be
   567→- When facing test failures with element selectors, add debug statements to print the actual HTML, but use `LazyHTML` selectors to limit the output, ie:
   568→
   569→      html = render(view)
   570→      document = LazyHTML.from_fragment(html)
   571→      matches = LazyHTML.filter(document, "your-complex-selector")
   572→      IO.inspect(matches, label: "Matches")
   573→
   574→### Form handling
   575→
   576→#### Creating a form from params
   577→
   578→If you want to create a form based on `handle_event` params:
   579→
   580→    def handle_event("submitted", params, socket) do
   581→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(params))}
   582→    end
   583→
   584→When you pass a map to `to_form/1`, it assumes said map contains the form params, which are expected to have string keys.
   585→
   586→You can also specify a name to nest the params:
   587→
   588→    def handle_event("submitted", %{"user" => user_params}, socket) do
   589→      {:noreply, assign(socket, form: to_form(user_params, as: :user))}
   590→    end
   591→
   592→#### Creating a form from changesets
   593→
   594→When using changesets, the underlying data, form params, and errors are retrieved from it. The `:as` option is automatically computed too. E.g. if you have a user schema:
   595→
   596→    defmodule MyApp.Users.User do
   597→      use Ecto.Schema
   598→      ...
   599→    end
   600→
   601→And then you create a changeset that you pass to `to_form`:
   602→
   603→    %MyApp.Users.User{}
   604→    |> Ecto.Changeset.change()
   605→    |> to_form()
   606→
   607→Once the form is submitted, the params will be available under `%{"user" => user_params}`.
   608→
   609→In the template, the form form assign can be passed to the `<.form>` function component:
   610→
   611→    <.form for={@form} id="todo-form" phx-change="validate" phx-submit="save">
   612→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   613→    
   614→
   615→Always give the form an explicit, unique DOM ID, like `id="todo-form"`.
   616→
   617→#### Avoiding form errors
   618→
   619→**Always** use a form assigned via `to_form/2` in the LiveView, and the `<.input>` component in the template. In the template **always access forms this**:
   620→
   621→    <%!-- ALWAYS do this (valid) --%>
   622→    <.form for={@form} id="my-form">
   623→      <.input field={@form[:field]} type="text" />
   624→    
   625→
   626→And **never** do this:
   627→
   628→    <%!-- NEVER do this (invalid) --%>
   629→    <.form for={@changeset} id="my-form">
   630→      <.input field={@changeset[:field]} type="text" />
   631→    
   632→
   633→- You are FORBIDDEN from accessing the changeset in the template as it will cause errors
   634→- **Never** use `<.form let={f} ...>` in the template, instead **always use `<.form for={@form} ...>`**, then drive all form references from the form assign as in `@form[:field]`. The UI should **always** be driven by a `to_form/2` assigned in the LiveView module that is derived from a changeset
   635→
   636→
   637→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
