content.content:
1→# Workflow: Task Management 2→ 3→## Overview 4→ 5→A single, unified workflow covering the full lifecycle of a task in a 6→prioritized todo list. The user captures tasks, organizes them by priority 7→through drag-and-drop reordering, tracks completion, and removes tasks that are 8→no longer needed. 9→ 10→## User Goal 11→ 12→The user wants three things in equal measure: 13→ 14→1. **Rapid capture** -- quickly add tasks so nothing falls through the cracks. 15→2. **Clear prioritized visibility** -- always see an ordered list showing what 16→ to do next. 17→3. **Completion satisfaction** -- mark things done and watch the list shrink. 18→ 19→Success looks like: the user opens the app, immediately sees their prioritized 20→list, can add/edit/reorder/complete/delete tasks with minimal friction, and 21→leaves confident that everything important is tracked. 22→ 23→## Actors 24→ 25→- **User** -- A single person managing their own task list. There is no 26→ authentication and no support for multiple users. 27→ 28→## Events 29→ 30→| # | Event | Description | 31→|---|-------|-------------| 32→| 1 | TaskAdded | A new task was captured. Includes description and a default position at the bottom of the list. | 33→| 2 | TaskCompleted | A task was marked as done. It disappears from the active list. | 34→| 3 | TaskReopened | A completed task was set back to incomplete. It returns to the top of the active list (highest priority). | 35→| 4 | TaskDescriptionChanged | The description text of a task was edited. | 36→| 5 | TaskReprioritized | A task was moved to a new position in the list via drag and drop. | 37→| 6 | TaskDeleted | A task was permanently removed (after user confirmation). | 38→ 39→## Commands 40→ 41→| Command | Produces Event | Trigger | Inputs | Rejection Conditions | 42→|---------|---------------|---------|--------|---------------------| 43→| AddTask | TaskAdded | [+ Add] button or Enter key | description | Empty description | 44→| CompleteTask | TaskCompleted | Checkbox on active task | task_id | Already completed | 45→| ReopenTask | TaskReopened | [Reopen] button on completed task | task_id | Not completed | 46→| ChangeTaskDescription | TaskDescriptionChanged | Inline edit confirm (blur/Enter) | task_id, new_description | Empty or unchanged description | 47→| ReprioritizeTask | TaskReprioritized | Drag and drop to new position | task_id, new_position | Same position (no change) | 48→| DeleteTask | TaskDeleted | [x] button + confirmation dialog | task_id | User cancels confirmation | 49→ 50→## Read Models 51→ 52→### ActiveTaskList 53→ 54→Powers the main view. Answers: "What are my current tasks, in priority order?" 55→ 56→| Field | Source Event(s) | 57→|-------|----------------| 58→| tasks[].task_id | TaskAdded.task_id | 59→| tasks[].description | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | 60→| tasks[].position | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | 61→| completed_count | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1) | 62→ 63→**Filters:** Only tasks where completed = false AND deleted = false. 64→**Ordering:** By position ascending (highest priority first). 65→ 66→### CompletedTaskList 67→ 68→Powers the completed tasks view. Answers: "What tasks have I finished?" 69→ 70→| Field | Source Event(s) | 71→|-------|----------------| 72→| tasks[].task_id | TaskAdded.task_id | 73→| tasks[].description | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | 74→ 75→**Filters:** Only tasks where completed = true AND deleted = false. 76→**Ordering:** By completion time (most recently completed first), derived from 77→TaskCompleted event timestamp. 78→ 79→## Wireframes 80→ 81→### Active Task List (main view) 82→ 83→``` 84→+--------------------------------------------------+ 85→| My Tasks [Completed (3)] | 86→+--------------------------------------------------+ 87→| | 88→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 89→| | 90→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 91→| :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] | 92→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 93→| :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] | 94→| | 95→+--------------------------------------------------+ 96→ 97→Legend: 98→ :: = drag handle (for reordering via drag and drop) 99→ [v] = mark complete (checkbox) 100→ [x] = delete button 101→ [Completed (3)] = link to completed tasks view with count 102→``` 103→ 104→### Inline Editing 105→ 106→``` 107→+--------------------------------------------------+ 108→| My Tasks [Completed (3)] | 109→+--------------------------------------------------+ 110→| | 111→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 112→| | 113→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 114→| :: [Call the dentist office____] [v] [ x ] | 115→| ^--- inline editing active | 116→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 117→| :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] | 118→| | 119→+--------------------------------------------------+ 120→``` 121→ 122→### Delete Confirmation Dialog 123→ 124→``` 125→+--------------------------------------------------+ 126→| My Tasks [Completed (3)] | 127→+--------------------------------------------------+ 128→| | 129→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 130→| | Delete Task? | | 131→| | | | 132→| | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | 133→| | permanently removed. | | 134→| | | | 135→| | [Cancel] [Delete] | | 136→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 137→| | 138→+--------------------------------------------------+ 139→``` 140→ 141→### Completed Tasks View 142→ 143→``` 144→+--------------------------------------------------+ 145→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 146→+--------------------------------------------------+ 147→| | 148→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 149→| ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 150→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 151→| | 152→| (No drag handles -- completed tasks have no | 153→| priority order) | 154→| | 155→+--------------------------------------------------+ 156→ 157→Legend: 158→ ~~text~~ = strikethrough (visually indicates completion) 159→ [Reopen] = sets task back to incomplete, returns to top of active list 160→ [x] = delete button (with confirmation dialog) 161→``` 162→ 163→### Empty State 164→ 165→``` 166→+--------------------------------------------------+ 167→| My Tasks [Completed (0)] | 168→+--------------------------------------------------+ 169→| | 170→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 171→| | 172→| No tasks yet. Add one above! | 173→| | 174→+--------------------------------------------------+ 175→``` 176→ 177→## Automations 178→ 179→None. This is a purely user-driven workflow with no automatic reactions. 180→ 181→## Translations (External Integrations) 182→ 183→None. The application is entirely self-contained. 184→ 185→## Workflow Diagram 186→ 187→```mermaid 188→flowchart LR 189→ subgraph User["User"] 190→ ActiveView["Active Task List
(main view)"] 191→ CompletedView["Completed Tasks
(separate view)"] 192→ ConfirmDialog["Delete Confirmation
Dialog"] 193→ end 194→ 195→ subgraph Commands["Commands"] 196→ AddTask["AddTask"]:::command 197→ CompleteTask["CompleteTask"]:::command 198→ ReopenTask["ReopenTask"]:::command 199→ ChangeDesc["ChangeTaskDescription"]:::command 200→ Reprioritize["ReprioritizeTask"]:::command 201→ DeleteTask["DeleteTask"]:::command 202→ end 203→ 204→ subgraph Events["Events"] 205→ TaskAdded["TaskAdded"]:::event 206→ TaskCompleted["TaskCompleted"]:::event 207→ TaskReopened["TaskReopened"]:::event 208→ TaskDescChanged["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event 209→ TaskReprioritized["TaskReprioritized"]:::event 210→ TaskDeleted["TaskDeleted"]:::event 211→ end 212→ 213→ subgraph Views["Read Models"] 214→ ActiveList["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 215→ CompletedList["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 216→ end 217→ 218→ ActiveView -->|"type + Add"| AddTask 219→ ActiveView -->|"click checkbox"| CompleteTask 220→ ActiveView -->|"inline edit"| ChangeDesc 221→ ActiveView -->|"drag and drop"| Reprioritize 222→ ActiveView -->|"click x"| ConfirmDialog 223→ CompletedView -->|"click Reopen"| ReopenTask 224→ CompletedView -->|"click x"| ConfirmDialog 225→ ConfirmDialog -->|"confirm"| DeleteTask 226→ 227→ AddTask --> TaskAdded 228→ CompleteTask --> TaskCompleted 229→ ReopenTask --> TaskReopened 230→ ChangeDesc --> TaskDescChanged 231→ Reprioritize --> TaskReprioritized 232→ DeleteTask --> TaskDeleted 233→ 234→ TaskAdded --> ActiveList 235→ TaskCompleted --> ActiveList 236→ TaskCompleted --> CompletedList 237→ TaskReopened --> ActiveList 238→ TaskReopened --> CompletedList 239→ TaskDescChanged --> ActiveList 240→ TaskDescChanged --> CompletedList 241→ TaskReprioritized --> ActiveList 242→ TaskDeleted --> ActiveList 243→ TaskDeleted --> CompletedList 244→ 245→ ActiveList --> ActiveView 246→ CompletedList --> CompletedView 247→ 248→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 249→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 250→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 251→``` 252→ 253→## Vertical Slices 254→ 255→### Command Slices 256→ 257→1. [AddTask](slices/add-task.md) -- User adds a new task to the list 258→2. [CompleteTask](slices/complete-task.md) -- User marks a task as done 259→3. [ReopenTask](slices/reopen-task.md) -- User sets a completed task back to incomplete 260→4. [ChangeTaskDescription](slices/change-task-description.md) -- User edits a task's description 261→5. [ReprioritizeTask](slices/reprioritize-task.md) -- User reorders tasks via drag and drop 262→6. [DeleteTask](slices/delete-task.md) -- User permanently removes a task 263→ 264→### View Slices 265→ 266→7. [ActiveTaskList](slices/active-task-list.md) -- Read model for the main task list view 267→8. [CompletedTaskList](slices/completed-task-list.md) -- Read model for the completed tasks view 268→ 269→### Automation Slices 270→ 271→None. 272→ 273→### Translation Slices 274→ 275→None. 276→ 277→## Business Rules 278→ 279→- New tasks are always added to the bottom of the active list (lowest priority). 280→- Reopened tasks are always placed at the top of the active list (highest priority). 281→- Completed tasks are hidden from the active list and visible in a separate completed view. 282→- Deletion requires user confirmation via a dialog. 283→- Deleted tasks cannot be restored from the UI (though events are preserved in the stream). 284→- Tasks can be deleted from either the active list or the completed tasks view. 285→- Task descriptions cannot be empty. 286→ 287→## Key Design Decisions 288→ 289→| Decision | Rationale | 290→|----------|-----------| 291→| New tasks go to bottom | Avoids disrupting existing priority order; user can reprioritize afterward | 292→| Reopened tasks go to top | If re-opening, the task is likely urgent or important | 293→| Completed tasks hidden by default | Keeps main list focused on what needs doing | 294→| Separate completed view | Allows review and reopening without cluttering the active list | 295→| Delete confirmation required | Prevents accidental permanent removal | 296→| Delete available in both views | User should not have to navigate between views to clean up | 297→| Inline description editing | Minimal friction; no modal or separate screen needed | 298→| Single list, no categories | Deliberately minimal; priority expressed only through ordering | 299→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→# Slice: AddTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user types a task description and submits it. A new task is created at the 10→bottom of the active list with the given description and a default position. 11→ 12→## Diagram 13→ 14→```mermaid 15→flowchart LR 16→ UI["Active Task List
input field + Add button"] 17→ CMD["AddTask"]:::command 18→ EVT["TaskAdded"]:::event 19→ VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 20→ 21→ UI -->|"description"| CMD 22→ CMD --> EVT 23→ EVT --> VIEW 24→ 25→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 26→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 27→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 28→``` 29→ 30→## Command: AddTask 31→ 32→| Field | Type | Description | 33→|-------|------|-------------| 34→| description | text | What needs to be done (non-empty) | 35→ 36→**Triggered by:** User types in the input field and clicks [+ Add] or presses 37→Enter. 38→ 39→**Rejection conditions:** 40→- Description is empty or blank. 41→ 42→## Event: TaskAdded 43→ 44→| Field | Type | Description | 45→|-------|------|-------------| 46→| task_id | identifier | Unique identifier assigned to the new task | 47→| description | text | The task description provided by the user | 48→| position | number | Position in the list (bottom / lowest priority) | 49→ 50→## Wireframe 51→ 52→``` 53→+--------------------------------------------------+ 54→| My Tasks [Completed (0)] | 55→+--------------------------------------------------+ 56→| | 57→| [ Buy groceries_________________] [+ Add] | 58→| ^--- user types here | 59→| | 60→| No tasks yet. Add one above! | 61→| | 62→+--------------------------------------------------+ 63→ 64→After submitting: 65→ 66→+--------------------------------------------------+ 67→| My Tasks [Completed (0)] | 68→+--------------------------------------------------+ 69→| | 70→| [ What needs to be done? ] [+ Add] | 71→| | 72→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 73→| | 74→+--------------------------------------------------+ 75→``` 76→ 77→## Read Model Impact 78→ 79→**ActiveTaskList:** New task appears at the bottom of the list. 80→ 81→## GWT Scenarios 82→ 83→### Scenario: Add task to an empty list 84→ 85→**Given** (prior events): 86→- (none -- no tasks exist yet) 87→ 88→**When** (command): 89→- AddTask { description: "Buy groceries" } 90→ 91→**Then** (events produced): 92→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 93→ 94→--- 95→ 96→### Scenario: Add task to a list with existing tasks (new task goes to bottom) 97→ 98→**Given** (prior events): 99→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 100→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 101→ 102→**When** (command): 103→- AddTask { description: "Fix the leaky faucet" } 104→ 105→**Then** (events produced): 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 107→ 108→--- 109→ 110→### Scenario: Add task when some tasks have been completed (position based on active + completed count) 111→ 112→**Given** (prior events): 113→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 114→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 115→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } 116→ 117→**When** (command): 118→- AddTask { description: "Write thank-you notes" } 119→ 120→**Then** (events produced): 121→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-004", description: "Write thank-you notes", position: 3 } 122→ 123→**Note:** Empty/blank description is prevented by the type system (NonEmptyString domain type), not by command rejection. No GWT scenario is needed for that case. 124→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.content:
1→# Slice: ActiveTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects all events into an ordered list of active
    10→(incomplete, non-deleted) tasks. This powers the main view of the application.
    11→
    12→## Diagram
    13→
    14→```mermaid
    15→flowchart LR
    16→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    17→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    18→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    19→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    20→    E5["TaskReprioritized"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E5 --> VIEW
    29→    E6 --> VIEW
    30→
    31→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    32→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    33→```
    34→
    35→## Read Model: ActiveTaskList
    36→
    37→### Fields
    38→
    39→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    40→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    41→| tasks | list | (all events) | Ordered list of active tasks |
    42→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    43→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    44→| tasks[].position | number | TaskAdded.position, TaskReprioritized.position, TaskReopened (top) | Current priority position |
    45→| completed_count | number | TaskCompleted (+1), TaskReopened (-1), TaskDeleted (if completed, -1) | Count of completed tasks for badge |
    46→
    47→### Filters
    48→
    49→- Only tasks where **completed = false** AND **deleted = false**.
    50→
    51→### Ordering
    52→
    53→- By position ascending. Position 1 = highest priority (top of list).
    54→
    55→### Event Processing Rules
    56→
    57→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    58→|-------|---------------------|
    59→| TaskAdded | Add new task at the bottom of the list (highest position number) |
    60→| TaskCompleted | Remove task from active list. Increment completed_count. |
    61→| TaskReopened | Add task back to active list at position 1 (top). Shift existing tasks down. Decrement completed_count. |
    62→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task. |
    63→| TaskReprioritized | Move task to new position. Shift other tasks to accommodate. |
    64→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from active list. If task was completed, decrement completed_count. |
    65→
    66→### Information Completeness Check
    67→
    68→| Display Element | Source |
    69→|----------------|--------|
    70→| Task description text | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    71→| Task order | TaskAdded.position / TaskReprioritized.position / TaskReopened (top) |
    72→| Completed count badge | TaskCompleted / TaskReopened / TaskDeleted |
    73→| Empty state message | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    74→
    75→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    76→
    77→## Wireframe
    78→
    79→```
    80→+--------------------------------------------------+
    81→|  My Tasks                        [Completed (3)]  |
    82→+--------------------------------------------------+
    83→|                                                    |
    84→|  [ What needs to be done?            ] [+ Add]    |
    85→|                                                    |
    86→|  ::  Buy groceries                    [v] [ x ]   |
    87→|  ::  Call the dentist                 [v] [ x ]   |
    88→|  ::  Fix the leaky faucet            [v] [ x ]   |
    89→|  ::  Write thank-you notes           [v] [ x ]   |
    90→|                                                    |
    91→+--------------------------------------------------+
    92→```
    93→
    94→## GWT Scenarios
    95→
    96→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of an empty list
    97→
    98→**Given** (current projection state):
    99→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [], completed_count: 0 }
   100→
   101→**When** (event to process):
   102→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }
   103→
   104→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   105→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   106→
   107→---
   108→
   109→### Scenario: TaskAdded adds task to the bottom of a populated list
   110→
   111→**Given** (current projection state):
   112→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 0 }
   113→
   114→**When** (event to process):
   115→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }
   116→
   117→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   118→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   119→
   120→---
   121→
   122→### Scenario: TaskCompleted removes task from active list and increments completed count
   123→
   124→**Given** (current projection state):
   125→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }
   126→
   127→**When** (event to process):
   128→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   129→
   130→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   131→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→### Scenario: TaskReopened adds task to top of list and decrements completed count
   136→
   137→**Given** (current projection state):
   138→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 2 }], completed_count: 2 }
   139→
   140→**When** (event to process):
   141→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }
   142→
   143→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   144→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 1 }
   145→
   146→**Note:** The reopened task's description ("Call the dentist") must be resolved from prior events. Existing tasks shift down (positions increment by 1).
   147→
   148→---
   149→
   150→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of an active task
   151→
   152→**Given** (current projection state):
   153→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   154→
   155→**When** (event to process):
   156→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office" }
   157→
   158→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   159→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Call the dentist office", position: 1 }], completed_count: 0 }
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a completed task does not affect active list
   164→
   165→**Given** (current projection state):
   166→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   167→
   168→**When** (event to process):
   169→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist office" }
   170→
   171→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   172→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   173→
   174→---
   175→
   176→### Scenario: TaskReprioritized moves task to a new position (higher priority)
   177→
   178→**Given** (current projection state):
   179→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   180→
   181→**When** (event to process):
   182→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 }
   183→
   184→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   185→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 2 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 3 }], completed_count: 0 }
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes an active task from the list
   190→
   191→**Given** (current projection state):
   192→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }, { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 }], completed_count: 1 }
   193→
   194→**When** (event to process):
   195→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   196→
   197→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   198→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   199→
   200→---
   201→
   202→### Scenario: TaskDeleted for a completed task decrements completed count but does not change active list
   203→
   204→**Given** (current projection state):
   205→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 2 }
   206→
   207→**When** (event to process):
   208→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-005" }
   209→
   210→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   211→- ActiveTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }], completed_count: 1 }
   212→
   213→**Note:** The projection must track whether a deleted task was completed in order to correctly adjust the completed_count.
   214→

content.content:
1→# Slice: CompletedTaskList
     2→
     3→## Pattern
     4→
     5→**View** (State View / Read Model)
     6→
     7→## Summary
     8→
     9→A read model that projects events into a list of completed (done, non-deleted)
    10→tasks. This powers the completed tasks view where users can review finished work
    11→and reopen or delete tasks.
    12→
    13→## Diagram
    14→
    15→```mermaid
    16→flowchart LR
    17→    E1["TaskAdded"]:::event
    18→    E2["TaskCompleted"]:::event
    19→    E3["TaskReopened"]:::event
    20→    E4["TaskDescriptionChanged"]:::event
    21→    E6["TaskDeleted"]:::event
    22→    VIEW["CompletedTaskList"]:::view
    23→
    24→    E1 --> VIEW
    25→    E2 --> VIEW
    26→    E3 --> VIEW
    27→    E4 --> VIEW
    28→    E6 --> VIEW
    29→
    30→    classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff
    31→    classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff
    32→```
    33→
    34→## Read Model: CompletedTaskList
    35→
    36→### Fields
    37→
    38→| Field | Type | Source Event(s) | Description |
    39→|-------|------|----------------|-------------|
    40→| tasks | list | (filtered events) | List of completed tasks |
    41→| tasks[].task_id | identifier | TaskAdded.task_id | Unique task identifier |
    42→| tasks[].description | text | TaskAdded.description, TaskDescriptionChanged.description | Current description (latest value) |
    43→
    44→### Filters
    45→
    46→- Only tasks where **completed = true** AND **deleted = false**.
    47→
    48→### Ordering
    49→
    50→- By completion time descending (most recently completed first). Completion
    51→  time is derived from the TaskCompleted event timestamp.
    52→
    53→### Event Processing Rules
    54→
    55→| Event | Effect on Read Model |
    56→|-------|---------------------|
    57→| TaskAdded | No effect (new tasks are not completed). |
    58→| TaskCompleted | Add task to completed list. |
    59→| TaskReopened | Remove task from completed list. |
    60→| TaskDescriptionChanged | Update description of matching task (if present in list). |
    61→| TaskDeleted | Remove task from completed list (if present). |
    62→
    63→### Information Completeness Check
    64→
    65→| Display Element | Source |
    66→|----------------|--------|
    67→| Task description text (with strikethrough) | TaskAdded.description / TaskDescriptionChanged.description |
    68→| Task ordering (most recent first) | TaskCompleted event timestamp |
    69→| Empty state | Derived: tasks list is empty |
    70→
    71→All fields trace to events. No missing data.
    72→
    73→## Wireframe
    74→
    75→```
    76→+--------------------------------------------------+
    77→|  [< Back to Tasks]              Completed Tasks   |
    78→+--------------------------------------------------+
    79→|                                                    |
    80→|  ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    81→|  ~~Send the invoice~~           [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    82→|  ~~Order new lightbulbs~~       [Reopen]  [ x ]   |
    83→|                                                    |
    84→+--------------------------------------------------+
    85→```
    86→
    87→## GWT Scenarios
    88→
    89→### Scenario: TaskAdded has no effect on completed list
    90→
    91→**Given** (current projection state):
    92→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
    93→
    94→**When** (event to process):
    95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 }
    96→
    97→**Then** (resulting projection state):
    98→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
    99→
   100→---
   101→
   102→### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds task to the completed list
   103→
   104→**Given** (current projection state):
   105→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [] }
   106→
   107→**When** (event to process):
   108→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" }
   109→
   110→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   111→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   112→
   113→**Note:** The task's description must be resolved from prior events (TaskAdded or TaskDescriptionChanged).
   114→
   115→---
   116→
   117→### Scenario: TaskCompleted adds most recent completion to the top
   118→
   119→**Given** (current projection state):
   120→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   121→
   122→**When** (event to process):
   123→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" }
   124→
   125→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   126→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   127→
   128→**Note:** Most recently completed task appears first (descending completion time).
   129→
   130→---
   131→
   132→### Scenario: TaskReopened removes task from completed list
   133→
   134→**Given** (current projection state):
   135→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   136→
   137→**When** (event to process):
   138→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" }
   139→
   140→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   141→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   142→
   143→---
   144→
   145→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged updates description of a completed task
   146→
   147→**Given** (current projection state):
   148→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   149→
   150→**When** (event to process):
   151→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }
   152→
   153→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   154→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries and snacks" }] }
   155→
   156→---
   157→
   158→### Scenario: TaskDescriptionChanged for a non-completed task does not affect completed list
   159→
   160→**Given** (current projection state):
   161→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   162→
   163→**When** (event to process):
   164→- TaskDescriptionChanged { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet soon" }
   165→
   166→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   167→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   168→
   169→---
   170→
   171→### Scenario: TaskDeleted removes task from completed list
   172→
   173→**Given** (current projection state):
   174→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }, { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   175→
   176→**When** (event to process):
   177→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" }
   178→
   179→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   180→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist" }] }
   181→
   182→---
   183→
   184→### Scenario: TaskDeleted for an active (non-completed) task does not affect completed list
   185→
   186→**Given** (current projection state):
   187→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   188→
   189→**When** (event to process):
   190→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-003" }
   191→
   192→**Then** (resulting projection state):
   193→- CompletedTaskList { tasks: [{ task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries" }] }
   194→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→# Slice: ReopenTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks [Reopen] on a completed task. The task is set back to 10→incomplete and placed at the top of the active list (highest priority position). 11→ 12→## Diagram 13→ 14→```mermaid 15→flowchart LR 16→ UI["Completed Tasks View
Reopen button"] 17→ CMD["ReopenTask"]:::command 18→ EVT["TaskReopened"]:::event 19→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 20→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 21→ 22→ UI -->|"task_id"| CMD 23→ CMD --> EVT 24→ EVT --> VIEW1 25→ EVT --> VIEW2 26→ 27→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 28→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 29→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 30→``` 31→ 32→## Command: ReopenTask 33→ 34→| Field | Type | Description | 35→|-------|------|-------------| 36→| task_id | identifier | The completed task to reopen | 37→ 38→**Triggered by:** User clicks the [Reopen] button on a task in the completed 39→tasks view. 40→ 41→**Rejection conditions:** 42→- Task is not completed (already active). 43→ 44→## Event: TaskReopened 45→ 46→| Field | Type | Description | 47→|-------|------|-------------| 48→| task_id | identifier | The task that was reopened | 49→ 50→**Business rule:** The reopened task is placed at the top of the active list 51→(highest priority position). This differs from newly added tasks which go to 52→the bottom. Rationale: if a user reopens a task, it is likely urgent. 53→ 54→## Wireframe 55→ 56→``` 57→Before (Completed Tasks View): 58→ 59→+--------------------------------------------------+ 60→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 61→+--------------------------------------------------+ 62→| | 63→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 64→| ~~Send the invoice~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 65→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 66→| | 67→+--------------------------------------------------+ 68→ 69→User clicks [Reopen] on "Send the invoice": 70→ 71→+--------------------------------------------------+ 72→| [< Back to Tasks] Completed Tasks | 73→+--------------------------------------------------+ 74→| | 75→| ~~Pick up dry cleaning~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 76→| ~~Order new lightbulbs~~ [Reopen] [ x ] | 77→| | 78→+--------------------------------------------------+ 79→ 80→Active Task List now shows (navigating back): 81→ 82→+--------------------------------------------------+ 83→| My Tasks [Completed (2)] | 84→+--------------------------------------------------+ 85→| | 86→| :: Send the invoice [v] [ x ] | 87→| ^--- reopened task at TOP | 88→| :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] | 89→| :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] | 90→| | 91→+--------------------------------------------------+ 92→``` 93→ 94→## Read Model Impact 95→ 96→**ActiveTaskList:** Task appears at the top (highest priority). Completed count 97→decremented. 98→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed from completed list. 99→ 100→## GWT Scenarios 101→ 102→### Scenario: Reopen a completed task (goes to top of active list) 103→ 104→**Given** (prior events): 105→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 107→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-002" } 108→ 109→**When** (command): 110→- ReopenTask { task_id: "task-002" } 111→ 112→**Then** (events produced): 113→- TaskReopened { task_id: "task-002" } 114→ 115→--- 116→ 117→### Scenario: Reject reopening a task that is already active 118→ 119→**Given** (prior events): 120→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 121→ 122→**When** (command): 123→- ReopenTask { task_id: "task-001" } 124→ 125→**Then** (error -- no events): 126→- Error: "Task is not completed" 127→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

You are facilitating architecture decisions for a Todo List application. The event model is complete and you need to guide the user through technology stack, domain boundaries, integration approaches, and cross-cutting concerns.

**Project path:** /home/jwilger/projects/todo_list
**Output:** docs/ARCHITECTURE.md

**IMPORTANT: Ask the user ONE question at a time. Use the user-input-protocol: write your question to /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jwilger-projects-todo-list/d9d5490d-b136-4afa-b591-23808bde2eba/scratchpad/user-input-request.md and then STOP and return, saying you need user input.**

**What already exists:**
- Phoenix 1.8.3 web framework (already in mix.exs)
- Ecto + Postgrex (already in mix.exs)  
- Phoenix LiveView 1.1.0 (already in mix.exs)
- Tailwind CSS v4 (already in mix.exs)
- esbuild (already in mix.exs)
- PostgreSQL 17 via Docker
- No CQRS/ES libraries yet (no Commanded, no EventStore)

**AGENTS.md confirms:** This is a Phoenix 1.8 app with LiveView, Ecto, Tailwind v4. Guidelines specify world-class UI, streams for collections, colocated JS hooks, and specific Elixir/Phoenix coding patterns.

**Domain context (from event model):**
- Single-user todo list (no auth)
- 6 events: TaskAdded, TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskReprioritized, TaskDeleted
- 6 commands: AddTask, CompleteTask, ReopenTask, ChangeTaskDescription, ReprioritizeTask, DeleteTask
- 2 read models: ActiveTaskList, CompletedTaskList
- No automations, no external integrations
- Deliberately minimal but intentionally over-engineered for demonstration purposes

**Architecture patterns to decide (from MEMORY — but present as options, don't assume):**
The project intends to use:
- Functional core / imperative shell pattern
- Effect pattern (domain returns Effect structs instead of doing IO)
- Domain types over primitives (TaskId, Description, Priority types)
- Commanded for CQRS/ES framework
- EventStore (postgres-based) for event persistence
- Ecto projections for read models

**Architecture decision areas to cover:**

1. **Event Sourcing Framework** — How will we implement CQRS/ES?
   - Options: Commanded library, custom implementation, etc.

2. **Event Store** — Where/how are events persisted?
   - Options: commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL-based), custom Ecto-based store, etc.

3. **Read Model Projections** — How do we build and maintain read models?
   - Options: Commanded projections (Ecto-based), custom GenServer projectors, etc.

4. **Domain Architecture** — How is the domain code organized?
   - Options: Functional core / imperative shell, traditional OOP-style contexts, etc.
   - Effect pattern vs direct side effects

5. **UI Architecture** — How does LiveView interact with the domain?
   - Real-time updates, streams, etc.

6. **Testing Strategy** — How do we test each layer?

For EACH decision area:
- Present 2-3 options with tradeoffs
- Ask the user to choose
- Ask ONE question at a time

After all decisions are made, write docs/ARCHITECTURE.md with:
- Project overview
- Architecture diagram (Mermaid)
- Technology stack
- Domain boundaries
- Each decision with rationale
- File structure
- Testing strategy

1→# Slice: ReprioritizeTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user drags a task from one position in the active list and drops it at a 10→new position. The task's priority changes relative to the other tasks. 11→ 12→## Diagram 13→ 14→```mermaid 15→flowchart LR 16→ UI["Active Task List
drag handle"] 17→ CMD["ReprioritizeTask"]:::command 18→ EVT["TaskReprioritized"]:::event 19→ VIEW["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 20→ 21→ UI -->|"task_id, new_position"| CMD 22→ CMD --> EVT 23→ EVT --> VIEW 24→ 25→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 26→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 27→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 28→``` 29→ 30→## Command: ReprioritizeTask 31→ 32→| Field | Type | Description | 33→|-------|------|-------------| 34→| task_id | identifier | The task being moved | 35→| new_position | number | The target position in the list | 36→ 37→**Triggered by:** User grabs a drag handle (::) and drops the task at a new 38→position in the active list. 39→ 40→**Rejection conditions:** 41→- Task dropped in the same position (no change). 42→ 43→## Event: TaskReprioritized 44→ 45→| Field | Type | Description | 46→|-------|------|-------------| 47→| task_id | identifier | The task that was moved | 48→| position | number | The new position in the priority-ordered list | 49→ 50→## Wireframe 51→ 52→``` 53→Before: 54→ 55→ :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] 56→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 57→ :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] 58→ :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] 59→ 60→User drags "Fix the leaky faucet" to the top: 61→ 62→ :: Fix the leaky faucet [v] [ x ] <-- moved here 63→ :: Buy groceries [v] [ x ] 64→ :: Call the dentist [v] [ x ] 65→ :: Write thank-you notes [v] [ x ] 66→``` 67→ 68→## Read Model Impact 69→ 70→**ActiveTaskList:** Task order updated. The moved task appears at its new 71→position; other tasks shift to accommodate. 72→ 73→## GWT Scenarios 74→ 75→### Scenario: Move a task to a higher priority (lower position number) 76→ 77→**Given** (prior events): 78→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 79→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 80→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 81→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-004", description: "Write thank-you notes", position: 4 } 82→ 83→**When** (command): 84→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-003", new_position: 1 } 85→ 86→**Then** (events produced): 87→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-003", position: 1 } 88→ 89→--- 90→ 91→### Scenario: Move a task to a lower priority (higher position number) 92→ 93→**Given** (prior events): 94→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 95→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 96→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-003", description: "Fix the leaky faucet", position: 3 } 97→ 98→**When** (command): 99→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-001", new_position: 3 } 100→ 101→**Then** (events produced): 102→- TaskReprioritized { task_id: "task-001", position: 3 } 103→ 104→--- 105→ 106→### Scenario: Reject reprioritizing to the same position (no change) 107→ 108→**Given** (prior events): 109→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 110→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 111→ 112→**When** (command): 113→- ReprioritizeTask { task_id: "task-002", new_position: 2 } 114→ 115→**Then** (error -- no events): 116→- Error: "Task is already at this position" 117→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

1→# Slice: DeleteTask 2→ 3→## Pattern 4→ 5→**Command** (State Change) 6→ 7→## Summary 8→ 9→The user clicks the delete button on a task (from either the active list or the 10→completed tasks view). A confirmation dialog appears. If the user confirms, the 11→task is permanently removed from all views. 12→ 13→## Diagram 14→ 15→```mermaid 16→flowchart LR 17→ UI1["Active Task List
delete button"] 18→ UI2["Completed Tasks View
delete button"] 19→ DIALOG["Confirmation Dialog"] 20→ CMD["DeleteTask"]:::command 21→ EVT["TaskDeleted"]:::event 22→ VIEW1["ActiveTaskList"]:::view 23→ VIEW2["CompletedTaskList"]:::view 24→ 25→ UI1 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 26→ UI2 -->|"click x"| DIALOG 27→ DIALOG -->|"confirm, task_id"| CMD 28→ CMD --> EVT 29→ EVT --> VIEW1 30→ EVT --> VIEW2 31→ 32→ classDef command fill:#3b82f6,color:#fff 33→ classDef event fill:#f59e0b,color:#fff 34→ classDef view fill:#22c55e,color:#fff 35→``` 36→ 37→## Command: DeleteTask 38→ 39→| Field | Type | Description | 40→|-------|------|-------------| 41→| task_id | identifier | The task to delete | 42→ 43→**Triggered by:** User clicks [x] on any task (active or completed), then 44→clicks [Delete] in the confirmation dialog. 45→ 46→**Rejection conditions:** 47→- User clicks [Cancel] in the confirmation dialog (command is never issued). 48→ 49→## Event: TaskDeleted 50→ 51→| Field | Type | Description | 52→|-------|------|-------------| 53→| task_id | identifier | The task that was deleted | 54→ 55→**Business rule:** Deletion is permanent from the UI perspective. The event 56→stream retains the full history, but the UI provides no way to restore a 57→deleted task. 58→ 59→## Wireframe 60→ 61→``` 62→User clicks [x] on "Fix the leaky faucet": 63→ 64→+--------------------------------------------------+ 65→| | 66→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 67→| | Delete Task? | | 68→| | | | 69→| | "Fix the leaky faucet" will be | | 70→| | permanently removed. | | 71→| | | | 72→| | [Cancel] [Delete] | | 73→| +--------------------------------------------+ | 74→| | 75→+--------------------------------------------------+ 76→ 77→If user clicks [Delete], the task disappears from the list. 78→If user clicks [Cancel], nothing changes. 79→``` 80→ 81→## Read Model Impact 82→ 83→**ActiveTaskList:** Task removed (if it was active). Remaining tasks may shift 84→positions. 85→**CompletedTaskList:** Task removed (if it was completed). 86→ 87→## GWT Scenarios 88→ 89→### Scenario: Delete an active task 90→ 91→**Given** (prior events): 92→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 93→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-002", description: "Call the dentist", position: 2 } 94→ 95→**When** (command): 96→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-002" } 97→ 98→**Then** (events produced): 99→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-002" } 100→ 101→--- 102→ 103→### Scenario: Delete a completed task 104→ 105→**Given** (prior events): 106→- TaskAdded { task_id: "task-001", description: "Buy groceries", position: 1 } 107→- TaskCompleted { task_id: "task-001" } 108→ 109→**When** (command): 110→- DeleteTask { task_id: "task-001" } 111→ 112→**Then** (events produced): 113→- TaskDeleted { task_id: "task-001" } 114→ 115→**Note:** The confirmation dialog is a UI concern. If the user clicks Cancel, the command is never issued, so there is no rejection scenario at the command level. Once the DeleteTask command is issued (after confirmation), it always succeeds. 116→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

You are facilitating architecture decisions for a Todo List application. The event model is complete and you need to guide the user through technology stack, domain boundaries, integration approaches, and cross-cutting concerns.

**Project path:** /home/jwilger/projects/todo_list
**Output:** docs/ARCHITECTURE.md

**IMPORTANT: Ask the user ONE question at a time. Use the user-input-protocol: write your question to /tmp/claude-1000/-home-jwilger-projects-todo-list/d9d5490d-b136-4afa-b591-23808bde2eba/scratchpad/user-input-request.md and then STOP and return, saying you need user input.**

**What already exists:**
- Phoenix 1.8.3 web framework (already in mix.exs)
- Ecto + Postgrex (already in mix.exs)  
- Phoenix LiveView 1.1.0 (already in mix.exs)
- Tailwind CSS v4 (already in mix.exs)
- esbuild (already in mix.exs)
- PostgreSQL 17 via Docker
- No CQRS/ES libraries yet (no Commanded, no EventStore)

**AGENTS.md confirms:** This is a Phoenix 1.8 app with LiveView, Ecto, Tailwind v4. Guidelines specify world-class UI, streams for collections, colocated JS hooks, and specific Elixir/Phoenix coding patterns.

**Domain context (from event model):**
- Single-user todo list (no auth)
- 6 events: TaskAdded, TaskCompleted, TaskReopened, TaskDescriptionChanged, TaskReprioritized, TaskDeleted
- 6 commands: AddTask, CompleteTask, ReopenTask, ChangeTaskDescription, ReprioritizeTask, DeleteTask
- 2 read models: ActiveTaskList, CompletedTaskList
- No automations, no external integrations
- Deliberately minimal but intentionally over-engineered for demonstration purposes

**Architecture patterns to decide (from MEMORY — but present as options, don't assume):**
The project intends to use:
- Functional core / imperative shell pattern
- Effect pattern (domain returns Effect structs instead of doing IO)
- Domain types over primitives (TaskId, Description, Priority types)
- Commanded for CQRS/ES framework
- EventStore (postgres-based) for event persistence
- Ecto projections for read models

**Architecture decision areas to cover:**

1. **Event Sourcing Framework** — How will we implement CQRS/ES?
   - Options: Commanded library, custom implementation, etc.

2. **Event Store** — Where/how are events persisted?
   - Options: commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL-based), custom Ecto-based store, etc.

3. **Read Model Projections** — How do we build and maintain read models?
   - Options: Commanded projections (Ecto-based), custom GenServer projectors, etc.

4. **Domain Architecture** — How is the domain code organized?
   - Options: Functional core / imperative shell, traditional OOP-style contexts, etc.
   - Effect pattern vs direct side effects

5. **UI Architecture** — How does LiveView interact with the domain?
   - Real-time updates, streams, etc.

6. **Testing Strategy** — How do we test each layer?

For EACH decision area:
- Present 2-3 options with tradeoffs
- Ask the user to choose
- Ask ONE question at a time

After all decisions are made, write docs/ARCHITECTURE.md with:
- Project overview
- Architecture diagram (Mermaid)
- Technology stack
- Domain boundaries
- Each decision with rationale
- File structure
- Testing strategy

input.content:
# Architecture Decision: Event Sourcing Framework

## Context

Your event model defines 6 commands, 6 events, and 2 read models -- all within a single "Task" aggregate. The application is already a Phoenix 1.8 project with Ecto and PostgreSQL. You need an approach for implementing CQRS and event sourcing.

## Question

How do you want to implement the CQRS/Event Sourcing layer?

## Options

### Option A: Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter

The Commanded library is the most mature CQRS/ES framework in the Elixir ecosystem. It provides:
- Aggregate lifecycle management (load from stream, apply events, persist)
- Command dispatching and routing
- Built-in process managers and event handlers
- `commanded_eventstore_adapter` uses a PostgreSQL-backed event store (separate DB schema)
- `commanded_ecto_projections` for building read models from events

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Battle-tested, well-documented, active community
- (+) Handles aggregate lifecycle, concurrency, snapshotting out of the box
- (+) Projection framework included
- (-) Opinionated -- you follow Commanded's patterns (aggregate modules, command structs, event structs)
- (-) Adds significant dependency surface (commanded, eventstore, projections, serializers)
- (-) The EventStore adapter requires its own PostgreSQL database (or schema), separate from your Ecto Repo

### Option B: Custom event sourcing on Ecto

Build a lightweight event sourcing layer directly on top of Ecto and your existing PostgreSQL database:
- An `events` table with stream_id, event_type, event_data (JSONB), version, inserted_at
- A pure-function aggregate module that folds events into state
- Command handling as plain module functions that validate + produce events
- Projections as Ecto schemas updated by event handlers (GenServer or simple function calls)

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Full control over every aspect -- no framework to learn or fight
- (+) Uses your existing Ecto Repo and PostgreSQL instance (no second DB)
- (+) Lighter dependency footprint
- (+) Easier to understand for readers of the codebase (everything is explicit)
- (-) You must build aggregate loading, optimistic concurrency, event persistence yourself
- (-) No built-in snapshotting, process managers, or subscription management
- (-) More code to write and maintain for infrastructure concerns

### Option C: Commanded with in-memory event store (for testing/learning)

Use Commanded but with `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory` for development, deferring the decision on persistent event storage:
- Same aggregate/command/event patterns as Option A
- Events only live in memory (lost on restart)
- Can swap to a real adapter later

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Fast to get started, no extra DB setup
- (+) Learn Commanded's patterns without infrastructure overhead
- (-) Cannot persist data across restarts (not suitable for even basic usage)
- (-) Defers rather than solves the storage question
- (-) Testing against in-memory may hide real issues with serialization, concurrency

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)

content.content:
1→# User Input Needed
     2→
     3→## Context
     4→Starting domain discovery for the Todo List project. I need to understand the business domain from scratch -- who uses this system, what they are trying to accomplish, and what the major processes are. I will not make assumptions about the domain even though this is a "todo list" -- the specifics matter.
     5→
     6→## Questions
     7→
     8→1. **What does this system do at a high level?** In your own words, what is the purpose of this application? What problem does it solve for people?
     9→
    10→2. **Who uses this system?** Are there different types of users or roles? (For example: is this a personal todo list for a single user, a shared/collaborative task system, or something else?)
    11→
    12→3. **What are the main things a user wants to accomplish?** Walk me through a typical interaction -- what does someone do when they open the application?
    13→
    14→4. **Are there any external systems this must integrate with?** (For example: email notifications, calendar sync, authentication providers, etc.) If so, which ones and for what purpose?
    15→
    16→5. **Is there anything unusual or specific about this todo list that differentiates it from a generic one?** Any particular business rules, constraints, or behaviors that are important to you?
    17→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

input.content:
# Architecture Decision 1 of 6: Event Sourcing Framework

## Context

Your event model defines 6 commands, 6 events, and 2 read models -- all within a single "Task" aggregate. The application is already a Phoenix 1.8 project with Ecto and PostgreSQL. You need an approach for implementing CQRS and event sourcing.

## Question

How do you want to implement the CQRS/Event Sourcing layer?

## Options

### Option A: Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter

The Commanded library is the most mature CQRS/ES framework in the Elixir ecosystem. It provides:
- Aggregate lifecycle management (load from stream, apply events, persist)
- Command dispatching and routing
- Built-in process managers and event handlers
- `commanded_eventstore_adapter` uses a PostgreSQL-backed event store (separate DB schema)
- `commanded_ecto_projections` for building read models from events

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Battle-tested, well-documented, active community
- (+) Handles aggregate lifecycle, concurrency, snapshotting out of the box
- (+) Projection framework included
- (-) Opinionated -- you follow Commanded's patterns (aggregate modules, command structs, event structs)
- (-) Adds significant dependency surface (commanded, eventstore, projections, serializers)
- (-) The EventStore adapter requires its own PostgreSQL database (or schema), separate from your Ecto Repo

### Option B: Custom event sourcing on Ecto

Build a lightweight event sourcing layer directly on top of Ecto and your existing PostgreSQL database:
- An `events` table with stream_id, event_type, event_data (JSONB), version, inserted_at
- A pure-function aggregate module that folds events into state
- Command handling as plain module functions that validate + produce events
- Projections as Ecto schemas updated by event handlers (GenServer or simple function calls)

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Full control over every aspect -- no framework to learn or fight
- (+) Uses your existing Ecto Repo and PostgreSQL instance (no second DB)
- (+) Lighter dependency footprint
- (+) Easier to understand for readers of the codebase (everything is explicit)
- (-) You must build aggregate loading, optimistic concurrency, event persistence yourself
- (-) No built-in snapshotting, process managers, or subscription management
- (-) More code to write and maintain for infrastructure concerns

### Option C: Commanded with in-memory event store (for testing/learning)

Use Commanded but with `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory` for development, deferring the decision on persistent event storage:
- Same aggregate/command/event patterns as Option A
- Events only live in memory (lost on restart)
- Can swap to a real adapter later

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Fast to get started, no extra DB setup
- (+) Learn Commanded's patterns without infrastructure overhead
- (-) Cannot persist data across restarts (not suitable for even basic usage)
- (-) Defers rather than solves the storage question
- (-) Testing against in-memory may hide real issues with serialization, concurrency

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)

input.content:
# Architecture Decision 2 of 6: Read Model Projections

## State

Decisions made so far:
1. **ES Framework:** Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL for dev/prod, in-memory for tests)

## Context

With Commanded chosen, you need to decide how your 2 read models (ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList) are built and maintained. Commanded offers a projection library, but there are alternatives.

## Question

How should read model projections be built and kept in sync with the event stream?

## Options

### Option A: commanded_ecto_projections

Use the `Commanded.Projections.Ecto` library. You define projector modules that subscribe to events and update Ecto schemas in your main PostgreSQL database.

- (+) First-party integration with Commanded -- handles subscriptions, error recovery, and idempotency
- (+) Projections live in your existing Ecto Repo (same DB as the rest of your app)
- (+) Well-documented pattern: one projector module per read model
- (-) Adds another dependency
- (-) Projections run asynchronously via subscriptions (eventual consistency between write and read)

### Option B: Commanded event handlers with manual Ecto updates

Use Commanded's built-in `Commanded.Event.Handler` to subscribe to events, but write your own Ecto Repo calls inside the handler (no projection library).

- (+) Fewer dependencies (no commanded_ecto_projections)
- (+) Full control over how projections are updated (custom queries, upserts, etc.)
- (+) Still uses Commanded's subscription infrastructure for delivery guarantees
- (-) You handle idempotency and error recovery yourself
- (-) More boilerplate per projector

### Option C: Inline projections during command dispatch

After each command succeeds and events are persisted, synchronously update the read model in the same process (no async subscription).

- (+) Strong consistency -- read model is always up to date immediately after a command
- (+) Simpler mental model (no eventual consistency to reason about)
- (+) No subscription infrastructure needed
- (-) Slower command processing (write + projection in one synchronous path)
- (-) Harder to add new projections later (must modify dispatch pipeline)
- (-) Does not scale if you add more read models or cross-aggregate projections

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)

Continue with the next architecture decision. Remember — ask ONE question at a time.

    36→- Projections as Ecto schemas updated by event handlers (GenServer or simple function calls)
    37→
    38→**Tradeoffs:**
    39→- (+) Full control over every aspect -- no framework to learn or fight
    40→- (+) Uses your existing Ecto Repo and PostgreSQL instance (no second DB)
    41→- (+) Lighter dependency footprint
    42→- (+) Easier to understand for readers of the codebase (everything is explicit)
    43→- (-) You must build aggregate loading, optimistic concurrency, event persistence yourself
    44→- (-) No built-in snapshotting, process managers, or subscription management
    45→- (-) More code to write and maintain for infrastructure concerns
    46→
    47→### Option C: Commanded with in-memory event store (for testing/learning)
    48→
    49→Use Commanded but with `Commanded.EventStore.Adapters.InMemory` for development, deferring the decision on persistent event storage:
    50→- Same aggregate/command/event patterns as Option A
    51→- Events only live in memory (lost on restart)
    52→- Can swap to a real adapter later
    53→
    54→**Tradeoffs:**
    55→- (+) Fast to get started, no extra DB setup
    56→- (+) Learn Commanded's patterns without infrastructure overhead
    57→- (-) Cannot persist data across restarts (not suitable for even basic usage)
    58→- (-) Defers rather than solves the storage question
    59→- (-) Testing against in-memory may hide real issues with serialization, concurrency
    60→
    61→## Decision Needed

# Architecture Decision 2 of 6: Read Model Projections

## State

Decisions made so far:
1. **ES Framework:** Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL for dev/prod, in-memory for tests)

## Context

With Commanded chosen, you need to decide how your 2 read models (ActiveTaskList and CompletedTaskList) are built and maintained. Commanded offers a projection library, but there are alternatives.

## Question

How should read model projections be built and kept in sync with the event stream?

## Options

### Option A: commanded_ecto_projections

Use the `Commanded.Projections.Ecto` library. You define projector modules that subscribe to events and update Ecto schemas in your main PostgreSQL database.

- (+) First-party integration with Commanded -- handles subscriptions, error recovery, and idempotency
- (+) Projections live in your existing Ecto Repo (same DB as the rest of your app)
- (+) Well-documented pattern: one projector module per read model
- (-) Adds another dependency
- (-) Projections run asynchronously via subscriptions (eventual consistency between write and read)

### Option B: Commanded event handlers with manual Ecto updates

Use Commanded's built-in `Commanded.Event.Handler` to subscribe to events, but write your own Ecto Repo calls inside the handler (no projection library).

- (+) Fewer dependencies (no commanded_ecto_projections)
- (+) Full control over how projections are updated (custom queries, upserts, etc.)
- (+) Still uses Commanded's subscription infrastructure for delivery guarantees
- (-) You handle idempotency and error recovery yourself
- (-) More boilerplate per projector

### Option C: Inline projections during command dispatch

After each command succeeds and events are persisted, synchronously update the read model in the same process (no async subscription).

- (+) Strong consistency -- read model is always up to date immediately after a command
- (+) Simpler mental model (no eventual consistency to reason about)
- (+) No subscription infrastructure needed
- (-) Slower command processing (write + projection in one synchronous path)
- (-) Harder to add new projections later (must modify dispatch pipeline)
- (-) Does not scale if you add more read models or cross-aggregate projections

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)

# Architecture Decision 3 of 6: Domain Architecture Pattern

## State

Decisions made so far:
1. **ES Framework:** Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL for dev/prod, in-memory for tests)
2. **Projections:** commanded_ecto_projections (Ecto schemas in main PostgreSQL database)

## Context

With Commanded handling the CQRS/ES infrastructure, you need to decide how to organize the domain logic itself -- specifically, how commands are validated, how aggregates produce events, and how side effects are managed. The event model has a single Task aggregate with 6 commands and 6 events.

## Question

How should the domain code be organized with respect to side effects and purity?

## Options

### Option A: Functional core / imperative shell with Effect pattern

Separate pure domain logic from side-effect-producing code:
- **Functional core** (`lib/todo_list/domain/`): Pure modules containing aggregate logic, command validation, event application, and domain types (TaskId, Description, Position). No IO, no Ecto, no Commanded -- just data in, data out.
- **Imperative shell** (`lib/todo_list/commanded/`): Thin adapter modules that wire the pure domain into Commanded's aggregate, router, and handler behaviours. The shell calls the core and translates results into Commanded's expected return values.
- **Effect pattern**: Domain functions return Effect structs (data describing what should happen) rather than performing side effects directly. The shell interprets and executes those effects.

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Domain logic is trivially testable (pure functions, no mocks)
- (+) Clear separation -- domain knows nothing about Commanded, Ecto, or Phoenix
- (+) Effect pattern makes side effects explicit and composable
- (+) Aligns with the project's learning/demonstration goals (showcases a disciplined architecture)
- (-) More files and indirection (domain types, effect structs, shell adapters)
- (-) Overkill for 6 commands on a single aggregate -- but that is the stated intent

### Option B: Commanded-native aggregates (domain logic inside Commanded modules)

Put domain logic directly in Commanded aggregate, command, and event modules:
- Commanded aggregate module contains `execute/2` and `apply/2` callbacks with business rules inline
- Command structs double as the domain command types
- Event structs double as domain event types
- No separate "domain" layer -- Commanded IS the domain layer

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Less indirection -- fewer files, simpler project structure
- (+) Follows Commanded's documented patterns exactly
- (+) Faster to implement for a small domain
- (-) Domain logic is coupled to Commanded's API (harder to test in isolation)
- (-) No clear boundary between "what the domain decides" and "how infrastructure executes it"
- (-) If you ever swap frameworks, the domain comes with it

### Option C: Phoenix context modules wrapping Commanded

Use Phoenix's conventional context pattern (e.g., `TodoList.Tasks`) as the public API, with Commanded wired internally:
- `TodoList.Tasks.add_task/1` dispatches a Commanded command internally
- `TodoList.Tasks.list_active/0` queries the Ecto projection
- Domain logic lives inside the context functions and Commanded aggregates
- LiveView talks only to the context module

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Familiar Phoenix pattern -- contexts as the API boundary
- (+) LiveView never knows about Commanded directly
- (-) Domain logic split between context functions and aggregate callbacks (unclear ownership)
- (-) Context becomes a pass-through layer with little value if Commanded already handles dispatch
- (-) Does not enforce purity -- contexts can mix domain logic with IO freely

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)

# Architecture Decision 3 of 6: Domain Architecture Pattern

## State

Decisions made so far:
1. **ES Framework:** Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL for dev/prod, in-memory for tests)
2. **Projections:** commanded_ecto_projections (Ecto schemas in main PostgreSQL database)

## Context

With Commanded handling the CQRS/ES infrastructure, you need to decide how to organize the domain logic itself -- specifically, how commands are validated, how aggregates produce events, and how side effects are managed. The event model has a single Task aggregate with 6 commands and 6 events.

## Question

How should the domain code be organized with respect to side effects and purity?

## Options

### Option A: Functional core / imperative shell with Effect pattern

Separate pure domain logic from side-effect-producing code:
- **Functional core** (`lib/todo_list/domain/`): Pure modules containing aggregate logic, command validation, event application, and domain types (TaskId, Description, Position). No IO, no Ecto, no Commanded -- just data in, data out.
- **Imperative shell** (`lib/todo_list/commanded/`): Thin adapter modules that wire the pure domain into Commanded's aggregate, router, and handler behaviours. The shell calls the core and translates results into Commanded's expected return values.
- **Effect pattern**: Domain functions return Effect structs (data describing what should happen) rather than performing side effects directly. The shell interprets and executes those effects.

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Domain logic is trivially testable (pure functions, no mocks)
- (+) Clear separation -- domain knows nothing about Commanded, Ecto, or Phoenix
- (+) Effect pattern makes side effects explicit and composable
- (+) Aligns with the project's learning/demonstration goals (showcases a disciplined architecture)
- (-) More files and indirection (domain types, effect structs, shell adapters)
- (-) Overkill for 6 commands on a single aggregate -- but that is the stated intent

### Option B: Commanded-native aggregates (domain logic inside Commanded modules)

Put domain logic directly in Commanded aggregate, command, and event modules:
- Commanded aggregate module contains `execute/2` and `apply/2` callbacks with business rules inline
- Command structs double as the domain command types
- Event structs double as domain event types
- No separate "domain" layer -- Commanded IS the domain layer

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Less indirection -- fewer files, simpler project structure
- (+) Follows Commanded's documented patterns exactly
- (+) Faster to implement for a small domain
- (-) Domain logic is coupled to Commanded's API (harder to test in isolation)
- (-) No clear boundary between "what the domain decides" and "how infrastructure executes it"
- (-) If you ever swap frameworks, the domain comes with it

### Option C: Phoenix context modules wrapping Commanded

Use Phoenix's conventional context pattern (e.g., `TodoList.Tasks`) as the public API, with Commanded wired internally:
- `TodoList.Tasks.add_task/1` dispatches a Commanded command internally
- `TodoList.Tasks.list_active/0` queries the Ecto projection
- Domain logic lives inside the context functions and Commanded aggregates
- LiveView talks only to the context module

**Tradeoffs:**
- (+) Familiar Phoenix pattern -- contexts as the API boundary
- (+) LiveView never knows about Commanded directly
- (-) Domain logic split between context functions and aggregate callbacks (unclear ownership)
- (-) Context becomes a pass-through layer with little value if Commanded already handles dispatch
- (-) Does not enforce purity -- contexts can mix domain logic with IO freely

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)

# Architecture Decision 4 of 6: Domain Types

## State

Decisions made so far:
1. **ES Framework:** Commanded + commanded_eventstore_adapter (PostgreSQL for dev/prod, in-memory for tests)
2. **Projections:** commanded_ecto_projections (Ecto schemas in main PostgreSQL database)
3. **Domain Architecture:** Commanded-native aggregates (domain logic inside Commanded modules, no separate domain layer)

## Context

The event model uses identifiers (task_id), text values (description), and numbers (position). These appear in command structs, event structs, and the aggregate state. You need to decide whether to use raw Elixir primitives (strings, integers) or wrap them in domain-specific types.

## Question

Should command/event fields use raw primitives or domain-specific value types?

## Options

### Option A: Domain-specific value types (structs wrapping primitives)

Define types like `TaskId`, `Description`, and `Position` as structs:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.TaskId do
  defstruct [:value]
  # value is a UUID string
end

defmodule TodoList.Task.Description do
  defstruct [:value]
  # value is a non-empty string, validated on creation
end
```

Commands and events use these types instead of raw strings/integers. Validation happens at the boundary when constructing the type (parse, don't validate).

- (+) Impossible to mix up a task_id string with a description string at the type level
- (+) Validation is centralized in the type constructor (e.g., Description rejects empty strings)
- (+) Self-documenting code -- function signatures express domain concepts
- (+) Aligns with "domain types over primitives" philosophy
- (-) More boilerplate (struct definitions, constructors, Jason encoding/decoding)
- (-) Commanded's event serialization needs custom handling (Jason.Encoder implementations)
- (-) Every boundary (LiveView params, Ecto projections) requires conversion to/from the type

### Option B: Raw primitives with validation in commands

Use plain strings and integers everywhere. Validate in the aggregate's `execute/2`:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.Commands.AddTask do
  defstruct [:task_id, :description]
  # task_id is a plain UUID string
  # description is a plain string
end
```

Validation logic lives in the aggregate (e.g., reject empty description in `execute/2`).

- (+) Simple, familiar, no boilerplate
- (+) Commanded serialization works out of the box (Jason encodes strings/integers natively)
- (+) No conversion needed at boundaries
- (-) Easy to accidentally pass wrong string to wrong field (no type safety)
- (-) Validation scattered across aggregate execute clauses
- (-) Less self-documenting -- you need to read the code to know what constraints apply

### Option C: Hybrid -- domain types for identifiers only

Use domain types for `TaskId` (where mix-ups are dangerous) but raw primitives for everything else:

```elixir
defmodule TodoList.Task.TaskId do
  defstruct [:value]
end

# But description is just a string, position is just an integer
```

- (+) Protects against the most dangerous mix-up (wrong ID) without full boilerplate
- (+) Less serialization overhead than full domain types
- (-) Inconsistent -- some fields are typed, others are not
- (-) Description validation still scattered

## Decision Needed

Which approach do you prefer? (A, B, or C -- or describe an alternative)
